title: Day 1 — Hello, Internet
instructions:
Day 1 — Hello, Internet

The one concept: a webpage is just text wrapped in [`<tags>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-tags).

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Claim your corner
  Level 2 — Name the tab, split the topic
  Level 3 — Build it like the pros
  Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive

Files you edit: index.html
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

<!-- 👋 Day 1: your first webpage!

     A webpage is just text wrapped in <tags>.
     Follow level1.md to get started.

     Preview loop: edit → save → refresh the browser. -->

<!-- Level 1 TODO: add a big heading (the h1 tag) with the name of
     something you love, then a paragraph (the p tag) about why it's great. -->

<!-- Level 3 hint: real pages have a skeleton. It's on the reference sheet:
     course/reference/html-reference.md -->

# Level 1 — Claim your corner Pick something you love. A game, a team, an animal, a band, a movie, a food. That's your **topic** — you'll use it all rotation. ## Do this 1. Open [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html). 2. Add a big title with [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1): ```html

Sourdough Bread

``` 3. Under it, add a paragraph with [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-p) — two or three sentences on why your thing is great: ```html

Sourdough is alive. The starter bubbles like a pet you can eat.

``` 4. Save, refresh the preview, admire. **Your anchors:** an `

` tag and a `

` tag in `index.html`. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] index.html exists and isn't empty - [ ] Has an `

` heading - [ ] Has a `

` paragraph

# Level 2 — Name the tab, split the topic Look at the browser tab. It's blank (or shows a file name). Real pages name their tab. ## Do this 1. Add a [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) tag with your page's name. Titles are invisible on the page — they live in the tab: ```html <title>All About Sourdough ``` *(Level 3 will move this into its proper home, [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-skeleton). For now, anywhere works.)* 2. Break your topic into **two sub-topics**, each with an [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h2) heading — smaller than [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), that's the point: ```html

Why It Beats Store Bread

My First Loaf Disaster

``` 3. Save → refresh. Notice the heading sizes. **Your anchors:** a `` with text inside, two `<h2>` tags. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has a `<title>` with actual text in it - [ ] Has at least two `<h2>` sub-headings

# Level 3 — Build it like the pros Every real webpage has the same skeleton. View-source any site: it's there. Time to build yours properly. ## Do this 1. Give your page the full skeleton (it's on the [reference sheet](reference/html-reference.md)): ```html All About Sourdough ...everything visible goes here... ``` Move your [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) into [`<head>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-skeleton). Everything visible moves into `<body>`. 2. Organize your page into **three [`<section>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-section) blocks** — one per sub-topic. Each section gets its own [`<h2>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h2) and its own [`<p>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-p): ```html <section> <h2>Why It Beats Store Bread</h2> <p>Flavour. Crust. Bragging rights.</p> </section> ``` (You'll need a third `<h2>` and a third `<p>` — three complete sections.) 3. Save → refresh → check the tab and the page. **Your anchors:** `<!doctype html>`, three `<section>` tags, three `<h2>`, three `<p>`. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Starts with `<!doctype html>` - [ ] Has three `<section>` blocks - [ ] Has three `<h2>` headings - [ ] Has three `<p>` paragraphs

# Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive

We taught you five tags today. HTML has over a hundred. Level 4 students find their own.

## Do this

Open the [HTML reference sheet](reference/html-reference.md) and scroll to the **Explorer Zone**. Add to your page:

1. **One text-effect tag** — something that changes how words look *inside* a paragraph:
   `<em>`, `<strong>`, or `<mark>`
2. **One structure tag** — something that adds a new kind of block to the page:
   `<hr>`, `<blockquote>`, `<table>`, or `<figure>`

No instructions beyond that. The reference sheet shows what each one does — pick the two that make *your* page better, figure out how they work, and use them somewhere that makes sense.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nobody showed you these. Reading a reference and teaching yourself a new tag is the actual job of a web developer — this is the skill, not the tag.

**Your anchors:** one of `em`/`strong`/`mark`, plus one of `hr`/`blockquote`/`table`/`figure`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] Uses a text-effect tag we never taught (`em`, `strong`, or `mark`)
- [ ] Uses a structure tag we never taught (`hr`, `blockquote`, `table`, or `figure`)

The bottom half — the **Explorer Zone** — is Level 4 territory: tags nobody will teach you, because finding them yourself is the skill. ## The skeleton (every page, every time) ```html Shows in the browser tab ...everything visible goes here... ``` ## Taught in class | Tag | What it is | Example | |---|---|---| | `

` | | `

` | | `

      ` | | `` | A link — `href` is the destination | `Go` | | `` | A link that jumps to an element on THIS page | `Games` | | `` | An image — `src` points at the file, `alt` describes it | `` | | `
      ` | One chunk of your page, usually with an `id` | `
      …
      ` | | `

      ` | The anatomy of a real page (Major 1's lesson) | — | | `` | | `` | A box users type into (Day 15) | `` | | `
      ` / `` | Plain boxes with no meaning — for styling | `
      …
      ` | ### All of it together The table shows tags one at a time. Here is what they look like **in a real page** — save this as `index.html` and it works: ```html All About Retro Games

      Retro Video Games

      Why Pixels Win Hall of Pain

      Why Pixels Win

      Pixel art never ages. A perfect sprite still looks perfect.

      Hall of Pain

      • Battletoads

      ``` Read it top to bottom: skeleton → header with a nav → main with two sections → footer. Every tag in the table above appears somewhere in there. ## 🔭 Explorer Zone Nobody teaches you these. That's the point. ### Text effects (inside paragraphs) | Tag | Does | |---|---| | `` | *Emphasis* (usually italics) | | `` | **Strong importance** (usually bold) | | `` | Highlighted like a highlighter pen | | `` | Fine print | | `` | Computer-looking text | ### Structure | Tag | Does | |---|---| | `

title: Day 2 — Lists on Lists
instructions:
Day 2 — Lists on Lists

The one concept: lists — [`<ul>`](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul) and [`<ol>`](http://localhost:3031/d02-ol) wrap [`<li>`](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) items (and lists can live inside lists).

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — The favourites list
  Level 2 — The Top 5
  Level 3 — Two lists, one real page
  Level 4 — 🧩 Puzzle: the list inside the list

Files you edit: index.html
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text: My Favourite Things

# Level 1 — The favourites list Your topic has favourites. Best flavours, best maps, best goals, best episodes — whatever your thing is, it has a top shelf. Today you list them properly, because bullet points are a *tag*, not a dash you type. ## Do this 1. Open [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html). The skeleton is pre-built today — you earned that yesterday. Make the [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) and [`<h1>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) yours. 2. Under the heading, add a bullet list. [`<ul>`](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul) wraps the whole list, [`<li>`](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) wraps each item: ```html <ul> <li>Mint chip</li> <li>Cookie dough</li> <li>Mango sorbet</li> </ul> ``` 3. At least **three** items — more is fine. Favourites from *your* topic, obviously, not mine. 4. Save → refresh. Real bullets, and you never typed a single dot. **Your anchors:** a `<ul>` with at least three `<li>` items in `index.html`. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] index.html exists and isn't empty - [ ] Has a `<ul>` bullet list - [ ] Has at least three `<li>` items

# Level 2 — The Top 5 A bullet list says "here are some things." A **numbered** list says "I have ranked these things and I will defend my choices." Time to publish your topic's official Top 5. ## Do this 1. Decide your Top 5. Best boss fights, best goals of the season, best flavours — the ranking is yours, and yes, people will argue. That's the fun part. 2. Under your bullet list, add an [`
    `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ol) — same shape as [`
      `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul), different wrapper: ```html
      1. Mint chip — refreshing, elite, undefeated
      2. Cookie dough
      3. Mango sorbet
      4. Plain vanilla (respect the classics)
      5. Bubblegum (someone had to be last)
      ``` 3. **Five items minimum.** Notice you never typed a number — `
        ` counts for you. 4. Prove the magic: move your #5 line to the top, save, refresh. Renumbered. For free. Now put it back (or don't — your ranking). **Your anchors:** an `
          ` with at least five [`
        1. `](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) items. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has an `
            ` numbered list - [ ] Your `
              ` has at least five `
            1. ` items

# Level 3 — Two lists, one real page Right now you've got two lists floating in space. Day 1 taught you how pros organize a page — sections with headings. Time to combine yesterday's skill with today's. ## Do this 1. Right under your [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), write a short [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-p) intro — what is this page, and why should anyone care: ```html

Ice cream is the only food with zero bad days. These are my picks.

``` 2. Build **two [`
`](http://localhost:3031/d01-section) blocks**, each with its own [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h2). Your bullet list lives in one, your Top 5 in the other: ```html

Flavours I Actually Buy

    ...your bullet list...

The Official Top 5

    ...your ranking...
``` 3. Count every [`
  • `](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) on the page — the checker wants **eight or more**. Three favourites + a Top 5 = eight, so you might already be there. Thin? Add more items — lists are cheap, opinions are free. 4. Save → refresh: intro, headings, bullets, numbers. That's a real page about your thing. **Your anchors:** a `

    ` intro, two `

    ` blocks, two `

    ` headings, one [`
      `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul) + one [`
        `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ol), eight total `
      1. `. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has two `
        ` blocks - [ ] Has two `

        ` headings - [ ] Uses both list types (`
          ` and `
            `) - [ ] Has a `

            ` intro paragraph - [ ] Has at least eight `

          1. ` items in total

# Level 4 — 🧩 Puzzle: the list inside the list Every menu with a sub-menu, every folder with folders inside it — that's a **nested list**: a list item that contains a whole other list. Nobody demoed this. That's the puzzle. ## The puzzle Turn one of your [`
  • `](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) items into a **category** with sub-items. "Cones" isn't one thing — sugar, waffle, cake — so "Cones" becomes an item that *contains its own list*, two levels deep. The entire trick is **where the inner list goes**: inside the item, *before* that item's closing tag. ```
  • Cones ...a whole new list lives HERE, before this item closes...
    • ``` Rules: - The inner list is a complete [`
      `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul) or [`
        `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ol) with **at least two** `
      1. ` sub-items of its own. - The category comes from *your* topic: a game and its bosses, a team and its lines, an album and its best tracks. - Indent the inner list further than its parent — future-you needs to see the layers. ⚠️ The classic trap: closing the outer `
        2. ` *before* the inner list starts. The browser shrugs and shows almost the same thing — the checker knows the difference. > 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** the browser forgives sloppy nesting, so this only passes if you truly get that tags *wrap*. Placing one closing tag in exactly the right spot proves more understanding than a page of copy-paste ever could. **Your anchors:** an `
      2. ` containing a `
          ` or `
            `, with two or more `
          1. ` sub-items inside it. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has a list nested inside a list item - [ ] The nested list has at least two sub-items

title: Day 3 — Pointing at Things
instructions:
Day 3 — Pointing at Things

The one concept: tags can carry a destination — [`href`](http://localhost:3031/d03-a) points a link somewhere, [`src`](http://localhost:3031/d03-img) points an image at a file.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Send them somewhere
  Level 2 — Show, don't just tell
  Level 3 — Build the jump menu
  Level 4 — 🔗 Combine: the clickable picture

Files you edit: index.html
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
My Topic

One or two sentences about your thing, so the page has a voice.

# Level 1 — Send them somewhere A page with no links is a dead end. The web is only a *web* because pages point at each other — and one tag does all of it. ## Do this 1. Open [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html). Make the [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) and [`<h1>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) about your topic (same topic as Day 1 and 2 — keep the streak). 2. Find a real website about your thing: the official site, a wiki page, a team page, a fan site. Copy the address out of the browser's address bar. It should start with `https://`. 3. Add a link. [`<a>`](http://localhost:3031/d03-a) is the tag, `href` is the destination, and the words *between* the tags are what people click: ```html <p>Everything I know came from <a href="https://www.si.edu/spotlight/skateboarding">the Smithsonian's skateboard page</a>.</p> ``` 4. Save → refresh → **click your link**. If it goes nowhere, check that the address is complete and still inside its quotes. > ⚠️ `<a>Cool Site</a>` with no `href` looks like plain text and goes nowhere. The destination is the whole point of the tag. **Your anchors:** an `<a>` tag in `index.html` whose `href` starts with `http`, with clickable words inside it. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] index.html exists and isn't empty - [ ] Has a link (an `<a>` tag with an href) - [ ] One link goes to a real site (href starts with http) - [ ] Your link has words you can click on

# Level 2 — Show, don't just tell Links point at *pages*. [`src`](http://localhost:3031/d03-img) points at *files* — and the file it usually points at is a picture. ## Do this 1. Find a picture of your thing and **save it into the same folder as your [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html)**. Give it a short, boring, lowercase filename: `board.jpg`, not `IMG_20240517 (2) final FINAL.jpg`. 2. Add the image. `` is a one-piece tag — there's no ``: ```html ``` 3. `src` is the path to the file. Same folder = just the filename. Tucked inside an [`images`](http://localhost:3031/d03-paths) folder = `images/board.jpg`. **Spelling and capitals count.** 4. Now the important half: **[`alt`](http://localhost:3031/d03-alt) is what a blind visitor hears instead of the picture.** Screen readers read it out loud. So describe what's actually in the photo: - ❌ `alt="pic1"` — tells them nothing - ❌ `alt="image"` — worse - ✅ `alt="A blue skateboard deck with a cracked nose"` — now they can picture it Aim for a short sentence, not a word. Ours needs **at least 10 characters** and it should be a real description of *your* picture. 5. Save → refresh. Broken image icon? Your `src` path is wrong 99% of the time. Check the spelling, check the folder, try again — that's debugging. **Your anchors:** an `` in `index.html` with a `src` that finds the file and an `alt` of 10+ real describing characters. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has an image with a src - [ ] Your image has real alt text (10+ characters describing it)

# Level 3 — Build the jump menu Long pages need a menu that takes you straight to the good part. Here's the trick: a link whose [`href`](http://localhost:3031/d03-a) starts with `#` doesn't leave your page — it **jumps to the element with that id**. Time to combine Day 1's sections, Day 2's lists, and today's links and images. ## Do this 1. Split your topic into **three [`
`](http://localhost:3031/d01-section) blocks**, each with an [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h2) and a paragraph — just like Day 1. 2. Give each section an [`id`](http://localhost:3031/d03-ids): a short, lowercase, no-spaces nickname. Dashes instead of spaces: ```html

Tricks I Can Actually Land

Two of them. Both are ollies.

``` 3. Up at the top, build a menu out of a [`
    `](http://localhost:3031/d02-ul) — three links, each pointing at one of your ids with a `#` in front: ```html
    • Tricks
    • Spots
    • Gear
    ``` The name after the `#` must match an `id` **exactly** — `#Tricks` will not find `id="tricks"`. 4. Add a **second image** somewhere in your sections, with its own honest [`alt`](http://localhost:3031/d03-alt) text. Two images minimum on the page, both described properly. 5. Save → refresh → click a menu link. The page should snap down to that section. (Short page? Add more paragraphs until it scrolls — then the jump is obvious.) **Your anchors:** three `
    `s with `id`s, three `` links that match those ids, two [``](http://localhost:3031/d03-img)s with 10+ character `alt` text. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] Has three jump links (href starting with #) - [ ] Has three `
    ` blocks - [ ] Has three elements with an id - [ ] A jump link points at an id that really exists - [ ] Has at least two images, each with real alt text

# Level 4 — 🔗 Combine: the clickable picture Every thumbnail you have ever clicked — every product photo, every video preview — is the same two tags you already know, stacked in an order nobody showed you. And it opens a second page, which means today you stop building *a page* and start building *a site*. ## The combine **Part 1 — an image that IS a link.** A link's clickable part is whatever sits between [``](http://localhost:3031/d03-a) and ``. So far you've put words there. Put a picture there instead: ``` ...the whole img tag goes HERE, between the two a tags... ``` The [``](http://localhost:3031/d03-img) goes *inside* the link. Nothing new to learn — just tags wrapping tags, exactly like Day 2's nested lists. **Part 2 — a second page.** In the same folder as [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), create a brand-new file called **[`more.html`](http://localhost:3031/d03-more-html)**. Give it the full skeleton and an [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), and fill it with the overflow: deep cuts, extra photos, the ranking that didn't fit. **Part 3 — wire them together.** - Your clickable image on `index.html` opens `more.html` (`href="more.html"` — no `https://`, no `#`, just the filename, because the file is right there beside it). - `more.html` has a link that goes back to `index.html`, so nobody gets stranded. Make it yours: a character portrait that opens their full profile, an album cover that opens the track ranking, a photo of your dog that opens the dog's biography. > 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nobody demoed a link wrapped around an image, and nobody told you how one file finds another. You had to work out that `href` doesn't only take web addresses — it takes *any* destination, including a file sitting next to yours. Round-trip navigation between two pages you built is, genuinely, a website. **Your anchors:** an `` wrapped directly around an ``; a file named `more.html` with an `

`; `index.html` links to `more.html` and `more.html` links back to `index.html`. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] An image is wrapped in a link (an `` around an ``) - [ ] more.html exists and isn't empty - [ ] more.html is a real page with an `

` - [ ] index.html links to more.html - [ ] more.html links back to index.html

title: Day 4 — Enter CSS
instructions:
Day 4 — Enter CSS

The one concept: a selector points a style at things. [`body`](http://localhost:3031/d04-aiming), [`h1`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), [`p`](http://localhost:3031/d01-p) — say which things, then say what happens to them.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Hit the whole page at once
  Level 2 — Aim smaller
  Level 3 — Invent your own selectors
  Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive

Files you edit: index.html, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
The Greatest Thing In The World

This page has no style at all. Black text, white background, Times New Roman. It looks like a printer threw up. Your job is to fix that without touching the words.

Why It's The Best

Three reasons, and the third one is the one that wins arguments. Replace this with your own take.

The whole story is over at Wikipedia, if you're the reading type.

The One Everybody Gets Wrong

Every topic has a take that people repeat because they heard it somewhere. Here's yours, corrected.

Where To Start

If someone wanted to get into this today, what's the first thing you'd hand them?

Built by me. Coloured by me, once style.css stops being empty.

/* 🎨 style.css — the day the internet gets its colours back.

   HTML says what things ARE. CSS says what they LOOK LIKE.
   Every rule you write has two parts: a SELECTOR (which things?)
   and a set of declarations (what should happen to them?).

   index.html is already wired to this file. Save this, refresh the
   page, and whatever you type here shows up. That's the loop. */


/* Level 1 TODO: restyle the entire page at once. There is a selector that
   means "the whole page" — point it at a new background colour and a
   different typeface. Recipe in level1.md. */


/* Level 2 TODO: stop styling everything the same. Give the big heading its
   own rule with its own colour, and give paragraphs a rule of their own
   that does something different. See level2.md. */


/* Level 3 TODO: invent your own labels. Three of them, plus one label used
   for a single special element that nothing else on the page may share.
   You'll be editing index.html for this one too. See level3.md. */


/* Level 4 TODO: two selector tricks nobody is going to teach you today.
   They're waiting in the Explorer Zone of the CSS reference sheet:
   course/reference/css-reference.md — see level4.md. */

# Level 1 — Hit the whole page at once

Today's starter is different: the page is already built and already wired to [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files). Open both files. [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html) is the words; `style.css` is empty and waiting.

First, spend two minutes swapping the placeholder words in `index.html` for **your** topic — you're about to decorate this thing, so decorate something you like. Then leave the HTML alone.

## Do this

1. In `style.css`, write your first rule. [`body`](http://localhost:3031/d04-aiming) is the selector that means *the entire page*:
   ```css
   body {
     background-color: #101820;
     font-family: Verdana, sans-serif;
   }
   ```
2. Read the shape out loud: **selector**, curly brace, then [`property: value;`](http://localhost:3031/d04-rule) lines, then a closing curly brace. Every CSS rule ever written looks like this.
3. Your `body` rule must set **both**:
   - a **background** — `background-color: #101820;` (a hex colour) or just `background-color: navy;` (a name)
   - a **font-family** — try `Georgia, serif`, `Verdana, sans-serif`, or `"Courier New", monospace`
4. Save `style.css` → refresh `index.html`. The *whole page* changes from one rule. That's aiming.
5. Now theme it your way: hunt for a colour that actually fits your topic. Horror movie page? Near-black and blood red. Sneakers? Concrete grey. Type any colour name you can think of and see if it exists — most do.

> ⚠️ Every declaration ends with a semicolon. Forget one and the rest of the rule quietly gives up.

**Your anchors:** a `body { }` rule in `style.css` containing a [`background`](http://localhost:3031/d04-properties) (or `background-color`) and a `font-family`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] style.css exists and isn't empty
- [ ] Your body rule sets a background
- [ ] Your body rule sets a font-family

# Level 3 — Invent your own selectors [`h1`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) and [`p`](http://localhost:3031/d01-p) were named by other people. Now you get to name things. A **class** is a label you invent, stick on as many elements as you want, and then aim at. An **id** is a label for exactly *one* special element — the star of the page. Today you finally go back into [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), because a label only works if both files agree on it. ## Do this 1. Pick three labels that mean something in *your* world: [`.bottle`](http://localhost:3031/d04-class), `.boss-fight`, `.verdict`, `.sad-fact`, `.legend`. Lowercase, no spaces, dashes instead of spaces. 2. Define them in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files). The dot means "this is a class": ```css .bottle { background-color: #451111; border: 2px solid orange; padding: 16px; } ``` You need **three class rules** in the stylesheet. 3. Stick the labels on elements in `index.html`. No dot here — just the name: ```html

Verdict: an all-timer.

``` You need **class= used at least three times** in the HTML. Same class on three different sections counts — that's the whole point of classes: write one rule, decorate many things. 4. Now the star. Pick the one element that deserves to stand out — your best section, your headline take — and give it an [`id`](http://localhost:3031/d03-ids) in `index.html`: ```html
``` Then aim at it in `style.css` with a `#`: ```css #hall-of-fame { border: 4px solid gold; } ``` **The name after the `#` must match the `id` exactly.** `#Hall-Of-Fame` will not find `id="hall-of-fame"`. 5. Save → refresh. One label, styled once, decorating your whole page. Theme it your way — a class named after your topic beats a class named `.thing1` every single time. > 💡 Class = a team jersey, as many players as you want. Id = a name tag, one person only. **Your anchors:** three `.class { }` rules in `style.css`, three `class="…"` attributes in `index.html`, one [`#id { }`](http://localhost:3031/d04-id-selector) rule with a matching `id="…"` in the HTML. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] style.css defines at least three class rules (.something) - [ ] index.html uses class= at least three times - [ ] style.css has an id rule (#something) - [ ] index.html has an element with an id

# Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive

Every selector you've written so far hits things wherever they are on the page. Real sites are pickier than that: "these links, but only the ones in the menu." And real sites *react* when your mouse arrives. Neither one got taught today.

## Do this

Open the [CSS reference sheet](reference/css-reference.md) and scroll to the **Explorer Zone**. Two things are hiding in there.

1. **A descendant selector** — two names with a space between them, which reads right-to-left: *this kind of thing, but only when it lives inside that kind of thing.*
   ```
   ...outer thing... ...inner thing... {  ...styles...  }
   ```
   The reference sheet shows you the real syntax. Aim it at something on your page that deserves special treatment: the links inside one section, the paragraphs inside your starred [`id`](http://localhost:3031/d03-ids), the headings inside a class.

   Then prove it works: the styled ones change, the identical elements *outside* that container don't. If everything changed, your selector isn't picky enough.

2. **A [`:hover`](http://localhost:3031/d07-hover) rule** — styles that only apply while the mouse is sitting on the element. Same rule shape, one extra bit stuck to the selector. Put it on a card, a section, or a link and give it a colour it doesn't normally have.

Rules:

- The descendant selector must be a **plain** one (two names, a space, then the brace) — a `:hover` on its own doesn't count as the descendant half.
- Both go in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files). No new HTML required, though you may add some.
- Theme it your way: a hover that suits your page. Horror site? It should get *worse* when you touch it.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** we only ever aimed at one thing at a time. A descendant selector means holding two ideas at once — *what* and *where* — and that's the mental jump from decorating a page to controlling one. Nobody showed you either of these; you read the docs and worked it out, which is what the job actually is.

**Your anchors:** one descendant selector (two names before the `{`) and one `:hover` rule in `style.css`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] Has a descendant selector (two names before the {)
- [ ] Has a :hover rule

title: Day 5 — Boxes All the Way Down
instructions:
Day 5 — Boxes All the Way Down

The one concept: every element is a box — [`border`](http://localhost:3031/d05-border) draws it, [`padding`](http://localhost:3031/d05-padding) pads it from the inside, [`margin`](http://localhost:3031/d05-margin) shoves everything else away.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Draw the box
  Level 2 — Give it room to breathe
  Level 3 — One class, worn by many
  Level 4 — 🧩 Puzzle: the box that parks itself

Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
My Topic

One line about why this topic runs my entire life.

Thing One

A couple of sentences here. Right now this text is squashed right up against the edge of everything. That is your problem to fix.

Thing Two

More sentences. Notice how nothing on this page has any room to breathe — every block is glued to the one above it.

Thing Three

Last one. Rename all of these headings so they are about your topic.

# Level 1 — Draw the box

Right now your page is a stack of naked text. Every element on it is secretly a rectangle — you just can't see the rectangles yet. Time to make one visible.

## Do this

1. Open [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files). The HTML is already built; today you mostly live in here.
2. Pick **one thing** to turn into a real box — your [`<h1>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) is the easy target — and give it a rule with **both** an outline and inside-space:
   ```css
   h1 {
     border: 3px solid hotpink;
     padding: 16px;
   }
   ```
3. Save → refresh. There's your box.
4. Now play with it, because this is the whole lesson:
   - Delete the [`padding`](http://localhost:3031/d05-padding) line and refresh. See how the words shove right up against the line?
   - Put it back and change it to `padding: 40px`. The box gets *bigger* — padding grows the box from the inside.
   - Try `border: 3px dashed` or `border: 6px double`. [`solid`](http://localhost:3031/d05-border), `dashed`, `dotted`, `double` all work.
5. Change the colours to something that suits your topic. Nothing here has to stay pink.

> 💡 Both properties have to be in the **same rule** — the same `{ }` pair. A border on one thing and padding on another doesn't count.

**Your anchors:** one rule in `style.css` containing a `border:` line and a `padding:` line.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] style.css exists and isn't empty
- [ ] index.html links the stylesheet
- [ ] One rule sets both `border:` and `padding:`

# Level 3 — One class, worn by many

You've got a look you like. Now imagine wanting it on six blocks. Copy-paste the same five lines six times? That's how you end up with a stylesheet nobody can fix — change your mind once and you're editing six places.

Pros write the look **once**, name it, and hang the name on whatever they want. That's a class.

## Do this

1. In [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files), build one rule called [`.card`](http://localhost:3031/d05-card) that uses **all five** box-model powers:
   ```css
   .card {
     border: 2px solid #f97316;
     border-radius: 12px;
     padding: 16px;
     margin: 20px;
     background: #1c1917;
   }
   ```
   [`border-radius`](http://localhost:3031/d05-shorthand) is the free win of the day — it rounds the corners. Try `4px`, then `24px`, then `999px` and enjoy yourself.
2. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), hang that name on **at least two** blocks:
   ```html
   <section class="card">
   ```
   Do it to all three sections if you want — that's the whole point of a class.
3. Save → refresh. Multiple identical cards, one rule.
4. Now the payoff: change `border-radius` to `24px` in that one rule and refresh. *Every* card changed. That's why classes exist.

> 💡 In CSS you write `.card` with a dot. In HTML you write `class="card"` with no dot. Mixing those up is the #1 reason a class "doesn't work."

**Your anchors:** a `.card` rule in `style.css` containing [`border`](http://localhost:3031/d05-border), [`padding`](http://localhost:3031/d05-padding), [`margin`](http://localhost:3031/d05-margin), `border-radius` and [`background`](http://localhost:3031/d04-properties) — and `class="card"` on two or more elements in `index.html`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] A `.card` rule with border, padding, margin, border-radius and a background
- [ ] The card class is used on at least two elements

# Level 4 — 🧩 Puzzle: the box that parks itself

Add one more box to your page — a narrow one. A warning label, a fun fact, a "milk, not water" panel, whatever fits your topic. Give it a **fixed width** so it doesn't stretch across the screen:

```css
.spotlight {
  width: 320px;
}
```

Refresh. It's narrow now — and it's jammed against the left edge like it owes the screen money.

## The puzzle

**Make that box sit dead centre — without typing a single space.**

Not [`text-align`](http://localhost:3031/d04-properties). That centres the *words inside* the box, not the box itself. Try it and watch it fail; that's part of the puzzle.

The real answer is **one word**, and it goes on a property you already used at Level 2. It's in the **Explorer Zone** of the [CSS reference sheet](reference/css-reference.md), under a heading that basically gives it away. Go find it.

Rules:

- The box needs a real [`width`](http://localhost:3031/d05-centre) — a number, not `100%`. A box already as wide as the page can't be centred; there's nowhere for it to go.
- The width and the centring trick have to be in the **same rule**.
- Style it like it belongs to your page: border, padding, radius, the works.

⚠️ Watch out for the near-miss: if the box still touches the left edge after your change, your `width` line is probably missing or set to a percentage.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** the obvious tool (`text-align`) is a trap, and the real answer isn't a new property — it's a *value* you'd never guess. Reading a reference sheet until the answer clicks, instead of waiting to be told, is the entire skill being tested here.

**Your anchors:** one rule in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files) containing both a `width:` and a [`margin`](http://localhost:3031/d05-margin) whose value includes `auto`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] A margin uses the word auto
- [ ] The same rule sets a width and an auto margin

One rule sets both border: and padding:
Uses margin to push things apart

A .card rule with border, padding, margin, border-radius and a background
The card class is used on at least two elements

title: Day 6 — Flexbox
instructions:
Day 6 — Flexbox

The one concept: flexbox — put [`display: flex`](http://localhost:3031/d06-display-flex) on a container and its children line up in a row.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Break the stack
  Level 2 — Steer the row
  Level 3 — The nav bar nobody has to be told about
  Level 4 — 🎯 Match the target

Files you edit: index.html, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
My Topic

First Second Third

Thing One

A card. Currently sitting on top of the other two like a pancake.

Thing Two

Another card. Same problem, one row lower.

Thing Three

The third pancake. These three want to be side by side.

# Level 1 — Break the stack

Open the page. Three cards, stacked like pancakes, wasting an entire screen of empty space on both sides. The browser did that because stacking is the only plan it has until you give it a better one.

Today you give it a better one. It's one property long.

## Do this

1. Look at [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html) first. Those three cards live inside a block: `<div class="card-row">`. That block is the **container**. Remember it.
2. In [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files), write a rule for the container:
   ```css
   .card-row {
     display: flex;
   }
   ```
3. Save → refresh. Three cards. One row. That's it — that's flexbox.
4. Now the experiment that makes it stick: move [`display: flex`](http://localhost:3031/d06-display-flex) from `.card-row` into [`.card`](http://localhost:3031/d05-card) and refresh. It breaks. Move it back.

> 💡 **The rule that saves you three weeks of pain:** `display: flex` goes on the **parent**, never on the children. You don't flex the cards — you flex the box that holds them. When flexbox "doesn't work," you have put it on the wrong element roughly 100% of the time.

Rename the headings and rewrite the card text for your topic while you're in there. Three cards about your thing beats three cards about "Thing One."

**Your anchors:** `display: flex` in `style.css`, on the container.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] style.css exists and isn't empty
- [ ] A container uses `display: flex`

# Level 2 — Steer the row

You have a row. It's fine. It's also glued together and shoved to the left like the cards are queuing for something. A row isn't a layout until you decide **where the items sit** and **how far apart they are**.

Two dials. Both go on the container, right next to your [`display: flex`](http://localhost:3031/d06-display-flex).

## Do this

1. Add a real gutter between the cards:
   ```css
   .card-row {
     display: flex;
     gap: 20px;
   }
   ```
   [`gap`](http://localhost:3031/d06-gap) only exists inside a flex container, and it beats margins because it never adds space on the outside edges.
2. Add the second dial and then go shopping — swap the value, refresh, repeat:
   ```css
   justify-content: center;
   ```
   Try all four and actually look at each one:
   - [`flex-start`](http://localhost:3031/d06-justify) — everything jammed left (the default)
   - `center` — the group sits in the middle
   - `space-between` — first item hard left, last hard right, gaps split evenly
   - `space-around` — everyone gets their own personal bubble
3. Pick the one that looks best for *your* three cards and keep it.
4. Bonus that costs one line: add [`flex: 1;`](http://localhost:3031/d06-flex-one) to your [`.card`](http://localhost:3031/d05-card) rule. Now the cards share the width equally instead of shrink-wrapping their text.

⚠️ Both properties go on `.card-row` — the container. Putting `justify-content` on `.card` does absolutely nothing, silently.

**Your anchors:** a `justify-content:` line and a `gap:` line in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files).

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] Uses justify-content to share out the leftover space
- [ ] Uses gap to put space between the items

# Level 4 — 🎯 Match the target

Six tiles. Build this.

```
┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐
│ 1  │ │ 2  │ │ 3  │
└────┘ └────┘ └────┘
┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐
│ 4  │ │ 5  │ │ 6  │
└────┘ └────┘ └────┘
```

That's the whole brief. No steps this time.

Things that are true about the target and are not up for negotiation:

- Six tiles, one shared class called `tile`, defined once in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files).
- They flow along a row and **spill onto the next line by themselves** — you are not allowed to build the second line by hand as a separate row. Squeeze the browser window narrower: a correct answer re-flows to 2 per line, then 1. A faked answer doesn't.
- Gaps between them, in both directions.

Fill the tiles with something from your topic — tricks, teams, songs, cards, bosses, flavours. Six of them. Emoji count.

The property you need is one line long, it lives on the container next to [`display: flex`](http://localhost:3031/d06-display-flex), and it is sitting in plain sight on the [CSS reference sheet](reference/css-reference.md). Nobody demoed it.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** you got a picture and no instructions. Reading a layout, working out which part is the container and which parts are the children, then hunting the one missing property in a reference sheet — that is *literally* the job. Being handed the steps is the training-wheels version.

**Your anchors:** [`flex-wrap: wrap`](http://localhost:3031/d06-wrap) in `style.css`, a `.tile` rule, and `class="tile"` on six elements in [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html).

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] Something uses `flex-wrap: wrap`
- [ ] A `.tile` rule is defined in style.css
- [ ] The tile class is used six times in index.html

Uses justify-content to share out the leftover space
Uses gap to put space between the items

title: Day 7 — Make It Slick
instructions:
Day 7 — Make It Slick

The one concept: the page can react to you — [`:hover`](http://localhost:3031/d07-hover) says *what changes*, [`transition`](http://localhost:3031/d07-transition) says *how fast*.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Make it notice you
  Level 2 — Make it glide
  Level 3 — Polish, everywhere, on purpose
  Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive: make it move on its own

Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
My Topic

First Second Third

Thing One

Point your mouse at this card. Notice that the card does not care.

Thing Two

Flat corners, no shadow, no reaction. It looks printed, not built.

Thing Three

By the end of today all three of these should light up under the mouse.

Read the guide Skip to the good part

# Level 1 — Make it notice you

Open the page and drag your mouse across everything. Nothing. Now do it on YouTube, or your school's site, or literally any site. Buttons light up, links change colour, things *acknowledge you*.

That gap is one selector wide.

## Do this

1. In [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files), find your `nav a` rule. Right underneath it, add a second rule — same selector, plus [`:hover`](http://localhost:3031/d07-hover):
   ```css
   nav a:hover {
     background: #38bdf8;
     color: #020617;
   }
   ```
2. Save → refresh → point at a nav link.
3. Read what you just wrote out loud, because this is the whole concept: `nav a` means "links in the nav." `nav a:hover` means "links in the nav, **but only while the mouse is on them**." Two rules, and the browser picks whichever one is currently true.
4. Play: hover-swap a colour, a background, a border colour. Try it on `.card:hover` too if you're curious — you'll want it at Level 3 anyway.

> 💡 `:hover` sticks to the end of a selector with no space. `nav a:hover` is right. `nav a :hover` is a different (and wrong) thing entirely.

Rename the nav links and headings for your topic while you're here.

**Your anchors:** a rule whose selector ends in `:hover`, setting [`color`](http://localhost:3031/d04-properties) or `background`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] style.css exists and isn't empty
- [ ] A `:hover` rule changes a colour or a background

# Level 4 — 🔭 Reference dive: make it move on its own

Everything you built today needs a mouse. Take the mouse away and the page goes back to being a poster.

Real sites have things that move with nobody touching them — a badge that bobs, a title that pulses, an arrow that nudges you downward forever. No mouse. And, surprisingly, **no JavaScript**.

## Do this

Open the [CSS reference sheet](reference/css-reference.md) and go to the **Explorer Zone**. There's a section in there about animation that we never mentioned once today. Read it until the two-part idea clicks — because that's the part that trips people:

1. You **define the movie** somewhere in your stylesheet, with a name you invent and what should be true at the start, the middle, and the end.
2. Then you **cast an element in it**, by name, on a normal rule — with a duration, and a word that means "do it again forever."

Both parts, or nothing happens. Defining an animation nobody is cast in does exactly what you'd expect: nothing.

Then:

- Add something small to [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html) worth animating — a badge next to your [`<h1>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), a medal, an emoji, a "NEW" flag. Give it a class.
- Make it move. The reference sheet lists what's worth animating: sliding up and down, tilting, growing, fading.
- Keep it subtle. A 6-pixel bob reads as *designed*. A spinning, flashing, sliding badge reads as *2003*.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** it's not one property — it's two pieces of syntax that only work as a pair, and one of them ([`@keyframes`](http://localhost:3031/d07-keyframes)) doesn't look like any CSS you've written all week. You have to read a reference, understand that a name you make up in one place gets used in another, and debug it when nothing moves. Nobody is going to walk you through it.

**Your anchors:** an `@keyframes` block with a name you chose, and an `animation:` line on a rule that uses that name.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] style.css defines an `@keyframes` animation
- [ ] Something on the page is cast in that animation with `animation:`

Uses transition so the change glides instead of snapping

The btn class is used on at least two elements
Cards react on hover
The .card rule itself carries a transition

# Band 1 — The frame stands

The minimum for a site that exists at all.

## Your site must have

1. **A real plan** in the [`<!-- PLAN: ... -->`](http://localhost:3031/m1-plan) comment — fill in all four lines. Actual thoughts, not "idk". The plan is marked.
2. The full skeleton (you've built it since Day 1) with a [`<title>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) naming your site.
3. [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files) linked, with at least a [`body`](http://localhost:3031/d04-aiming) rule so the page isn't default-ugly.
4. An [`<h1>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) with your site's name.
5. At least **two [`<section>`](http://localhost:3031/d01-section) blocks** of real content.

## ✅ Checks in this band

- [ ] index.html exists
- [ ] style.css exists
- [ ] The PLAN comment is filled in with real thinking
- [ ] The stylesheet is linked
- [ ] Has an h1 site name
- [ ] Has two sections of real content
- [ ] style.css styles the body

# Band 2 — Real anatomy Today's mini-lesson, applied. Your page gets the same bones as every professional site. ## Your site must have 1. A [`
`](http://localhost:3031/m1-anatomy) with your site name and a `
` menu. 2. A `
` holding **three [`
`](http://localhost:3031/d01-section) blocks**, each with an [`id`](http://localhost:3031/d03-ids): ```html
…
``` 3. A `
` — made by you, plus the year. 4. A working menu: **three links in the nav that jump to your sections**: ```html
Races My Events Why Swimming Wins
``` Click each one. If the page doesn't jump, the [`href`](http://localhost:3031/d03-a) and the `id` don't match. ## ✅ Checks in this band - [ ] Has header, nav, main, and footer - [ ] Has three sections - [ ] The nav has three links that jump to page sections (#…)

# Band 3 — Provincial standard 🎯 This is what "a real website" means. Everything from the CSS week, working together. ## Your site must have 1. **Three images, every one with real alt text** — alt text is how blind users experience your images; write what the picture *shows*: ```html ``` *(Save images into an `images/` folder in your dropbox. School-appropriate, as always.)* 2. **Classes doing real work** — at least three CSS classes you defined and use ([`.card`](http://localhost:3031/d05-card), `.event-list`, `.highlight`…). If two things look the same, they should share a class. 3. **Flexbox somewhere it earns its place** — the nav, a row of cards, a photo strip. 4. **A hover effect** — something responds when the mouse touches it. ## ✅ Checks in this band - [ ] Three images, all with alt text - [ ] Three classes defined in style.css - [ ] Classes are used in the page - [ ] Uses display: flex - [ ] Has a :hover effect

# Band 4 — 🚀 Above and beyond Two upgrades, both of which you'll have to figure out mostly on your own. ## 1. A second page Create [`extra.html`](http://localhost:3031/m1-band4) — a deeper dive on one part of your topic (a full match breakdown, a character guide, a recipe, a timeline). It needs: - The same skeleton and the same `` to your stylesheet (one stylesheet, two pages — that's the pro move) - A link **to** it from your index nav: `…` - A link **back** to [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html) so nobody gets stranded ## 2. A CSS animation The [CSS reference sheet](reference/css-reference.md) Explorer Zone covers [`@keyframes`](http://localhost:3031/d07-keyframes). Nobody taught you this — that's the point. Make something on your page move: a pulsing title, a floating badge, a slow-scrolling background. ```css @keyframes float { /* what happens at 0%, 50%, 100%? The reference sheet knows. */ } ``` Then attach it to an element with the `animation:` property. > 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** a multi-page site with shared styles and a self-taught animation is genuinely beyond Grade 9 standard. If you get here, you're not doing the course anymore — you're just building websites. ## ✅ Checks in this band - [ ] extra.html exists - [ ] index.html links to extra.html - [ ] extra.html links back to index.html - [ ] style.css has an @keyframes animation - [ ] Something on the page uses the animation

Day 10 — The Machine Awakens

The one concept: variables — the page can compute.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:

starterFiles.text:
The Machine Awakens

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — Wake the machine

Your page has a brain now: [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Anything you [`show()`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) appears in the output box.

## Do this

1. Open `script.js`. Find the **Level 1** section.
2. Show a message about your thing:
   ```js
   show("The sourdough zone is now ONLINE.");
   ```
3. Save → refresh → your words appear in the output box.
4. Add a second `show()` just to feel the power.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** a `show("...")` call in `script.js`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] script.js exists and isn't empty
- [ ] Calls show() with a message in quotes

# Level 2 — Labelled boxes

A variable is a labelled box. The starter has three empty boxes waiting for **your** thing — the labels are fixed (the checker looks for them), the values are 100% yours.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section and fill in the three variables:
   ```js
   const topic = "Sourdough bread";
   const rating = 9;
   const why = "It is the only pet you are allowed to eat.";
   ```
   - `topic` — text (in quotes)
   - `rating` — a number 1–10 (no quotes!)
   - `why` — one sentence of text
2. Show at least two of them **by name** — no quotes around the variable name:
   ```js
   show(topic);
   show(rating);
   ```

> 🔍 [`show(topic)`](http://localhost:3031/d10-quotes) prints what's *in the box*. `show("topic")` prints the word "topic". The quotes are the difference.

**Your anchors:** `topic`, `rating`, `why` filled in; [`show(...)`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) called with a variable.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] topic is filled in with text
- [ ] rating is a number greater than 0
- [ ] why is a real sentence
- [ ] show() is called with a variable (no quotes)

# Level 3 — Make the machine compute

Anyone can store a number. Level 3 makes the machine **calculate something you never typed**.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Turn `powerLevel` into a real calculation that *uses* `rating` (and any other numbers you invent):
   ```js
   const yearsAFan = 3;
   const powerLevel = rating * yearsAFan * 100;
   ```
   Other ideas: slices eaten per week × 52. Goals scored × difficulty. Episodes watched × minutes each.
2. `show(powerLevel);` — the machine did math *you* didn't.
3. Change `rating` at Level 2 and refresh. Watch `powerLevel` change **by itself**. That's the whole point of variables.

**Your anchors:** `powerLevel` computed from `rating` (or another variable) with `+`, `-`, `*`, or `/`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] powerLevel is a number and not 0
- [ ] powerLevel is calculated from a variable using + - * or /
- [ ] show() is called with powerLevel

# Level 4 — 🧩 The puzzles

No new tools. Just the variables you already know — and two problems that make you *think* with them.

## Puzzle A — The swap

The starter gives you:

```js
let a = 3;
let b = 7;
```

Make `a` hold `7` and `b` hold `3` — **without typing the numbers 3 or 7 anywhere below those lines.**

> 🤔 Try `a = b; b = a;` first. Watch what happens. (Spoiler: both boxes end up with the same thing — figure out *why*, and you've basically solved it.) Hint: a third box can hold a copy.
>
> 😈 Bonus flex (not checked): do it with *zero* extra boxes, using only math.

## Puzzle B — The minutes machine

The starter gives you `const totalMinutes = 137;`. Fill in:

- `hours` — how many **whole** hours fit in 137 minutes
- `minutesLeft` — how many minutes are left over

Your code must still work if someone changes `137` to any other number — so no typing `2` or `17` directly! The 🔭 [reference sheet](reference/js-reference.md) Explorer Zone has a tool for chopping off decimals.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** both puzzles use only the one thing you learned today — variables. No new tools, no recipe to copy. Working out *how* to move values around when the obvious way fails is the reasoning muscle every programmer runs on.

**Your anchors:** `a`, `b`, `hours`, `minutesLeft` — already declared in the starter, your code sets their values.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] a is 7 and b is 3 (the swap worked)
- [ ] hours is 2 and minutesLeft is 17 (computed, not typed)

starterFiles.text:
String Theory

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — A string is text in a box

Numbers were yesterday. Today the boxes hold **words** — and the checker only cares that the box is full, not what you love.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section.
2. Fill `intro` with one sentence about your thing:
   ```js
   const intro = "Welcome to the sourdough zone: bread with a personality.";
   ```
3. Show it **by name** on the next line:
   ```js
   show(intro);
   ```
4. Save → refresh → your sentence appears in the output box.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** `intro` filled in; `show(intro)` called.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] intro is filled in with text
- [ ] show() is called with intro

# Level 2 — Glue variables into a sentence

Gluing strings with `+` is a chore. **Template literals** let you pour variables straight into a sentence: backticks instead of quotes, [`${ }`](http://localhost:3031/d11-template) where a variable goes.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Invent **two or more** variables about your thing — any names, any values:
   ```js
   const flavour = "sourdough";
   const loavesPerWeek = 3;
   ```
2. Rebuild `combo` as a **template literal** that mixes at least two of them into one sentence:
   ```js
   let combo = `I bake ${loavesPerWeek} loaves of ${flavour} every single week.`;
   ```
   - Backticks `` ` ``, not quotes — the key is top-left, under Esc.
   - Each `${ }` is a slot; whatever variable you put inside gets dropped into the sentence.
3. `show(combo);` — save → refresh → the machine wrote your sentence.

> 🔍 Change one variable's value and refresh. The sentence updates *itself*. That's why template literals beat typing the sentence by hand.

**Your anchors:** `combo` rebuilt as a template literal with two `${ }` slots — your other variable names are yours.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] combo is a template literal with two ${ } slots
- [ ] combo is a real sentence (more than 10 characters)

# Level 3 — The mad-lib machine

Time to combine everything: a story-building machine that takes three words from a *player* and mashes them into your story. **A friend should be able to play your mad-lib** — that's the finish line.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Make `madLib` return **one story string** (a template literal!) that uses all three words it receives:
   ```js
   function madLib(word1, word2, word3) {
     return `A wild ${word1} ${word2} into the ${word3} and refused to leave.`;
   }
   ```
   Make the story *yours* — 30+ characters, the weirder the better. The words can land anywhere in it.
2. Below the function, collect three words with the [`prompt()`](http://localhost:3031/d11-prompt) trick from the mini-lesson:
   ```js
   const playerNoun = prompt("Give me an animal or a thing:");
   const playerVerb = prompt("Give me a past-tense action word:");
   const playerPlace = prompt("Give me a place:");
   ```
3. Feed them in and show the result — exactly like this shape:
   ```js
   show(madLib(playerNoun, playerVerb, playerPlace));
   ```
4. Refresh → answer the three pop-ups → your story appears. Now **trade seats with a neighbour** and play each other's. If they laugh, you've passed the real test.

> 🔍 The checker never answers your prompts — it calls `madLib` directly with secret words. So the story must come from the *parameters*, not from words you typed into the function.

**Your anchors:** `madLib(word1, word2, word3)` returning the story; three `prompt()` calls; `show(madLib(...))`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] madLib("dragon", "sneezed", "cafeteria") tells a story (30+ characters) using all three words
- [ ] madLib works with ANY three words (no hardcoding!)
- [ ] Three prompt() calls collect words from the player
- [ ] The finished story is shown with show(madLib(...))

# Level 4 — 🔭 The shout-o-meter

Strings come with built-in power tools we never mentioned in class. Time to build a **shout-o-meter**: one function that makes any word LOUD, and one that measures it.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** the lesson never taught these tools. The 🔭 [reference sheet](reference/js-reference.md) **Explorer Zone** lists the string methods — finding the right one and making it work is the whole challenge. That's what real developers do all day.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 4** section. Make `shout` give back the word in ALL CAPS with a `!` stuck on the end:
   - `shout("hi")` → `"HI!"`
   - `shout("wow")` → `"WOW!"`
   - The Explorer Zone has a method that upper-cases a string; the `+` glue you already know handles the `!`.
2. Make `letterCount` give back **how many letters** the word has:
   - `letterCount("pizza")` → `5`
   - `letterCount("a")` → `1`
   - The Explorer Zone has this one too — it's not even a method, just a property.
3. Point the shout-o-meter at your own words:
   ```js
   show(shout(flavour));
   ```
   No typing `"HI!"` by hand — the functions must work for **any** word, because the checker tests them with words you can't see.

**Your anchors:** `shout(word)`, `letterCount(word)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills them in.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] shout("hi") returns "HI!"
- [ ] shout("wow") returns "WOW!"
- [ ] letterCount("pizza") returns 5
- [ ] letterCount("a") returns 1

starterFiles.text:
Function Junction

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — A function is a named recipe

One recipe, endless greetings. The checker will call `greet` with names you've never seen — so the *recipe* has to do the work, not you.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section.
2. Make `greet` give back a greeting that **uses the name it receives** — yesterday's template literal is perfect:
   ```js
   function greet(name) {
     return `Yo ${name}, welcome to the bakery!`;
   }
   ```
   The greeting words are 100% yours — theme it your way. The only rule: `${name}` must appear somewhere in it.
3. Call it a few times and show the results:
   ```js
   show(greet("Ada"));
   show(greet("Grace"));
   ```
4. Save → refresh → two different greetings from **one** recipe.

> 🔍 If you typed a name *inside* the function (like `return "Hi Ada"`), every call gives the same answer — and the hidden-name test catches it instantly. Use the parameter.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** `greet(name)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills it in.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] greet("Ada") returns a real greeting that contains "Ada"
- [ ] greet("Zork") contains "Zork" (no hardcoding the name!)

# Level 2 — Numbers in, numbers out

Functions eat numbers too. [`double`](http://localhost:3031/d05-border) takes any number and hands back twice as much — the checker feeds it numbers you can't see, so no shortcuts exist.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Make `double` give back the number times two:
   ```js
   function double(n) {
     return n * 2;
   }
   ```
2. Prove it works on the page — theme the sentence around your thing:
   ```js
   show(`If 12 friends show up, we need ${double(12)} slices of pizza.`);
   ```
3. Try `double(double(5))` in a [`show()`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) just to see what happens. (It's 20. Functions stack.)

> 🔍 `return n * 2` works for *every* number ever. `return 8` works for exactly one test and fails the other — that's the difference between a recipe and an answer.

**Your anchors:** `double(n)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills it in.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] double(4) returns 8
- [ ] double(10) returns 20

# Level 3 — Two ingredients

Recipes can take **more than one** ingredient. `area` takes a width *and* a height — and with it your machine can size a dance floor, a minecraft build, a soccer pitch, a poster… anything rectangular in your world.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Make `area` give back width times height:
   ```js
   function area(width, height) {
     return width * height;
   }
   ```
   Order matters when you call it: `area(5, 4)` pours `5` into [`width`](http://localhost:3031/d05-centre) and `4` into `height`.
2. Use it on something from *your* thing, and show the result:
   ```js
   show(`The dance floor is ${area(5, 4)} square metres. Plenty of room.`);
   ```
3. Check the scoreboard: by now `greet`, [`double`](http://localhost:3031/d05-border), and `area` should **all** use [`return`](http://localhost:3031/d12-return) — three working recipes, zero copy-paste.

> 🔍 The checker calls `area` with sizes you've never seen. If your function returns `width * height`, you literally cannot lose.

**Your anchors:** `area(width, height)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills it in.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] area(3, 4) returns 12
- [ ] area(5, 2) returns 10
- [ ] script.js has at least 3 return statements

# Level 4 — 🧩 Functions calling functions

Here's the secret nobody tells beginners: **functions calling functions is how all real software is built.** Every app you use is thousands of small recipes calling each other. Today you build your first two-storey one.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nothing new to learn — the challenge is *composition*. `partyInvite` must not write its own hello; it must **call your `greet` function** and build on top of the answer. The checker reads your function's source, so faking it by copy-pasting the greeting text won't fly.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 4** section. Make `partyInvite` build its invitation **by calling `greet`**, then adding the day:
   ```js
   function partyInvite(name, day) {
     return `${greet(name)} Party at my place this ${day}. Bring snacks.`;
   }
   ```
   Theme it your way — bakery launch, championship watch party, capybara meetup. Two rules only: the invite **starts from `greet(name)`'s answer**, and the **day** appears in it.
2. Show an invite:
   ```js
   show(partyInvite("Ada", "Friday"));
   ```
3. The magic trick: change your greeting inside `greet`… and refresh. **Every invitation updates itself.** One recipe feeding another — that's the whole software industry in one line.

**Your anchors:** `partyInvite(name, day)` — already declared in the starter, must call `greet` inside.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] partyInvite("Ada", "Friday") contains your greet() greeting AND the day
- [ ] partyInvite calls greet( ) inside itself

starterFiles.text:
The Fork in the Road

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — One fork in the road

A message that always appears is boring. A message that appears **only when something is true** is a program making a decision.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section.
2. Invent a number variable about your thing — a rating, a score, a price, a hype level:
   ```js
   const hypeLevel = 9;
   ```
3. Put an [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if) underneath it that compares that number with `<` or `>`, and shows a message when the door opens:
   ```js
   show(`Hype level: ${hypeLevel}/10`);

   if (hypeLevel > 7) {
     show("LINE UP. The wait is worth it.");
   }
   ```
4. Save → refresh → the message appears. Now change the number so the condition is **false**, refresh again, and watch the message vanish. That's the whole concept, and you just proved it works.
5. Feeling it? Add a second `if` that catches the low numbers.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** an `if (... < ...)` or `if (... > ...)` in `script.js`; a [`show("...")`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) call.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] Has an if that compares two things with < or >
- [ ] Calls show() with a message in quotes

# Level 4 — 🧩 The leap year puzzle

February 29th only exists in some years. Most people think the rule is "every 4 years." Most people are wrong, and software has crashed over it — real, expensive, front-page crashes.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nobody shows you the code for this one. The rule has an exception, and then the exception has an exception — and turning tangled English into working logic *without a template to copy* is the actual job. You'll also need a tool the lesson never taught: a way to say "both of these" and "either of these" in one [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if). The 🔭 [reference sheet](reference/js-reference.md) Explorer Zone has it under **AND / OR**.

## The rule, in plain English

1. A year divisible by **4** is a leap year.
2. **Except** years divisible by **100** — those are *not* leap years.
3. **Except except:** years divisible by **400** *are* leap years after all.

So 2024 leaps. 1900 does not (divisible by 100). 2000 does (divisible by 400). 2023 is nowhere close.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 4** section and finish `isLeapYear(year)`. It hands back [`true`](http://localhost:3031/d13-else) or `false` — no printing, no quotes.
2. You need a way to ask "does this divide evenly?" The Explorer Zone's **remainder** operator (`%`) gives you what's left over after a division. If the leftover is `0`, it divided evenly. That's your whole toolkit.
3. Work out the answers on paper *first* for 2024, 1900, 2000, 2023. Then make the code agree with your paper.
4. Sanity-check on the page:
   ```js
   show(`1900 was a leap year? ${isLeapYear(1900)}`);
   ```

> 😈 Two routes get there: a stack of `if` / [`else if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-else-if) branches in the right order (rule 3 checked **before** rule 2, or it never fires), or one beefy condition using AND and OR. Both are legit. The one-liner is the flex.
>
> 🔍 The checker uses years you haven't seen, so hardcoding four answers won't survive.

**Your anchors:** `isLeapYear(year)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills it in.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] isLeapYear(2024) returns true
- [ ] isLeapYear(1900) returns false
- [ ] isLeapYear(2000) returns true
- [ ] isLeapYear(2023) returns false

Day 14 — Push the Button

The one concept: events — buttons make code run.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:

Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
Push the Button

Nothing has happened yet…

# Level 1 — The magic button

Three steps. Every interactive thing you'll ever build is these three steps.

## Do this

1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), add your button inside `#controls` (word it your way):
   ```html
   <button id="magic-button">Do not press</button>
   ```
2. In [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js), **inside the browser block**, wire it up:
   ```js
   const magic = document.getElementById("magic-button");
   magic.addEventListener("click", function () {
     document.getElementById("status").textContent = "YOU PRESSED IT. Everything is different now.";
   });
   ```
3. Save → refresh → press the button. Feel that? That's cause and effect, baby.

**Your anchors:** `id="magic-button"` in the HTML; [`addEventListener("click", ...)`](http://localhost:3031/d14-listen) and [`textContent`](http://localhost:3031/d14-text-content) in the script.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The page has a #magic-button
- [ ] Something listens for a click
- [ ] A click changes text with textContent

# Level 2 — Style on demand

Text is one thing a click can change. **Style** is another.

## Do this

1. Add a second button:
   ```html
   <button id="color-button">Make it weird</button>
   ```
2. Wire it (inside the browser block) to change a **style** instead of text:
   ```js
   const colorBtn = document.getElementById("color-button");
   colorBtn.addEventListener("click", function () {
     document.getElementById("page-title").style.color = "hotpink";
   });
   ```
3. Theme it your way: change the heading colour, the status border, the button's own background — any [`.style.`](http://localhost:3031/d14-style) change counts. Stack two or three changes in one click for maximum drama.

> 🔍 CSS property names change costume in JS: [`background-color`](http://localhost:3031/d04-properties) becomes `style.backgroundColor` — the dash becomes a capital letter.

**Your anchors:** `id="color-button"`; a `.style.` change in the script.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The page has a #color-button
- [ ] A click changes a style with .style.

# Level 4 — ⚡ The toggle

Two buttons for one job is amateur hour. Every real app has **one** switch that flips back and forth. The catch: the page has to *remember* which mode it's in. That's a variable + if/else — yesterday's skill powering today's.

## Do this

1. **First, the brain.** Outside the browser block, finish `nextTheme`:
   - `nextTheme("light")` returns `"dark"`
   - `nextTheme("dark")` returns `"light"`

   That's an if/else and two returns. The checker calls this function directly — make it perfect.
2. **Then, the button.** Add `<button id="toggle-button">` and wire it:
   ```js
   let theme = "light"; // the page's memory

   document.getElementById("toggle-button").addEventListener("click", function () {
     theme = nextTheme(theme);
     // TODO: if theme is now "dark", add the class; otherwise remove it.
   });
   ```
3. Click it five times. It should alternate every single time.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nothing here is new — but nobody showed you how to make a page *remember*. Combining state + decisions + events without a recipe is exactly what "above and beyond" means.

**Your anchors:** function `nextTheme` (tested directly!), `#toggle-button`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] nextTheme("light") returns "dark"
- [ ] nextTheme("dark") returns "light"
- [ ] The page has a #toggle-button
