title: Day 15 — Tools of the Trade
instructions:
Day 15 — Tools of the Trade

The one concept: input boxes — the upgrade from [`prompt()`](http://localhost:3031/d11-prompt).

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — The echo machine
  Level 2 — Numbers are different
  Level 3 — Build an actual tool
  Level 4 — 🛡️ The bouncer

Files you edit: index.html, script.js, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
Tools of the Trade

No pop-ups. No training wheels. Real controls only.

Your answer will appear right here.

# Level 1 — The echo machine

Simplest possible proof that the upgrade works: you type, you click, the page repeats you. No pop-up in sight.

## Do this

1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), inside `#controls`, add a box and a button:
   ```html
   <input id="user-input" type="text" placeholder="Type here…">
   <button id="go-button">Echo it back</button>
   ```
   The [`placeholder`](http://localhost:3031/d15-the-box) is the ghost text inside the box — make it match your theme.
2. In [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js), **inside the browser block**, wire it up. It's yesterday's three steps plus [`.value`](http://localhost:3031/d15-value):
   ```js
   const resultBox = document.getElementById("result");

   document.getElementById("go-button").addEventListener("click", function () {
     const typed = document.getElementById("user-input").value;
     resultBox.textContent = `"${typed}" — bold choice. Respect.`;
   });
   ```
3. Save → refresh → type → click. Your words land in `#result`, which was waiting for you in the starter.
4. Type something new and click again. It updates every time. **A prompt could never.**

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone. And notice one of them mentions `prompt` — that's the checker's business, not yours. Yours are retired.

**Your anchors:** `id="user-input"`, `id="go-button"` in the HTML; `.value` and [`textContent`](http://localhost:3031/d14-text-content) in the script.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The page has a text box with id user-input
- [ ] The page has a #go-button
- [ ] The script reads what was typed with .value
- [ ] The typed text is displayed with textContent

# Level 2 — Numbers are different

Here's the bug that has burned every programmer alive: **[`input.value`](http://localhost:3031/d15-value) is ALWAYS text.** Type `5` in a box and JavaScript hands you the *character* `"5"`, not the number 5. So [`"5" + "5"`](http://localhost:3031/d15-always-text) is `"55"`. [`Number()`](http://localhost:3031/d15-number) fixes that.

## Do this

1. Add a second box in [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), this one for numbers:
   ```html
   <input id="number-input" type="number" placeholder="How many?">
   ```
   [`type="number"`](http://localhost:3031/d15-type-number) gives you the little spinner arrows and a number pad on phones. It does **not** turn the value into a number. It's still text. This is not a typo — it's the whole level.
2. **See the bug with your own eyes first.** Wire a click that does this and look at the result:
   ```js
   const typed = document.getElementById("number-input").value;
   resultBox.textContent = typed + 10;
   ```
   Type `5`, click, and enjoy `"510"`. That's glue, not addition.
3. **Now fix it** with `Number()`:
   ```js
   const amount = Number(typed);
   resultBox.textContent = amount + 10;
   ```
   `5` + `10` = `15`. The machine is doing math again.
4. Say the rule out loud, because you'll need it every day from now on: **read it, then Number it, then do math.**

> 🔍 You can reuse your Level 1 button or add a second one — a second button (any id) keeps things tidy for Level 3.

**Your anchors:** `id="number-input"` with `type="number"`; `Number()` in the script.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The page has a number box with id number-input
- [ ] That box is set to type="number"
- [ ] The script converts text into a real number with Number()

# Level 4 — 🛡️ The bouncer

Go to your tool, leave the box **empty**, and click. Or type `banana`. Congratulations: your tool just told someone their total is `$NaN`. ([`NaN`](http://localhost:3031/d15-nan) = "Not a Number" — JavaScript's way of shrugging.)

Real software never shrugs at a user. It says what went wrong, politely, and lets them try again.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** every level so far assumed the user cooperates. This one assumes they don't — empty boxes, typos, `banana`. Defending against input you didn't expect is the difference between a class demo and something you'd let a stranger touch, and nobody handed you the recipe for it.

## Do this

1. **Build the bouncer, outside the browser block.** `validate(text)` takes the raw text from the box and hands back [`true`](http://localhost:3031/d13-else) or `false`:
   - `validate("")` → `false` — nothing typed
   - `validate("abc")` → `false` — not a number
   - `validate("12")` → `true` — let them through
2. Two things to figure out:
   - **Empty:** an empty box gives back `""`. Careful — someone can also type three spaces. `.trim()` shaves the spaces off the ends, and then you can test whether anything is left.
   - **Not a number:** `Number("abc")` gives you `NaN`. There's a built-in way to *ask* whether a value came out as `NaN` — `Number.isNaN(...)` — because weirdly, `NaN === NaN` is `false`. (Yes. That's real. JavaScript is like this sometimes.)
3. **Put the bouncer on the door,** inside the browser block. Check *before* the math, and show a friendly message in `#result` when the input is bad:
   ```js
   const typedText = document.getElementById("number-input").value;

   if (validate(typedText) === false) {
     resultBox.textContent = "Hold up — type a real number (digits only, and don't leave it empty).";
   } else {
     // your Level 3 math, safe at last
   }
   ```
   Make the message *yours* and make it kind. "ERROR 400 INVALID INPUT" is what a machine says. "Hold up — I need a number here!" is what a tool says.
4. Test it like you're trying to break it: empty. Spaces. `banana`. `12`. `3.5`. `-4`. Your tool should never print `NaN` again.

**Your anchors:** `validate(text)` returning `true`/`false`; a call to `validate` in the browser block that guards the math.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] validate("") returns false
- [ ] validate("abc") returns false
- [ ] validate("12") returns true
- [ ] The button actually calls validate before doing the math

The script converts text into a real number with Number()

title: Days 16–17 — MAJOR 2: Build-a-Tool
instructions:
Days 16–17 — MAJOR 2: Build-a-Tool

<!-- slides:start -->

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Band 1 — The frame stands
  Band 2 — It computes
  Band 3 — Provincial standard 🎯
  Band 4 — 🚀 Bulletproof

Files you edit: index.html, script.js, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
<!-- PLAN:
     Tool name:
     Who is it for:
     Inputs:
     What it figures out:
     One thing that will make it cool:
-->
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title><!-- your tool's name --></title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Build your tool here. Your plan above is the map.
         level1.md tells you where to start. -->
    <script src="script.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

/* Build-a-Tool — your stylesheet.
   Band 3 wants this page STYLED like you mean it: a body rule,
   classes doing real work, and a hover effect.
   Band 4 wants your plan's cool thing — make something move or glow
   when the tool answers (the CSS reference sheet has the goods). */

# Band 1 — The frame stands The minimum for a tool that exists at all: a plan, and a page with somewhere to type, something to press, and somewhere for the answer to land. ## Your tool must have 1. **A real plan** in the [``](http://localhost:3031/m1-plan) comment — fill in all five lines. Actual thoughts, not "idk". The plan is marked, and Band 4 will make you keep the promise on the last line. 2. An [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) with your tool's name (put it in the [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) too). 3. **Two labelled input boxes**, each with an [`id`](http://localhost:3031/d03-ids) you choose (kebab-case, named for what goes in them): ```html <label for="pack-price">Price of one pack ($)</label> <input id="pack-price" type="number"> ``` 4. The button that runs everything: ```html <button id="go-button">Run the numbers</button> ``` 5. An element where the answer will appear: ```html <p id="result">Your verdict shows up here…</p> ``` Nothing works yet. That's fine — Band 1 is the skeleton, Band 2 is the spark. **Your anchors:** two `<input>` ids (your choice of names), `id="go-button"`, `id="result"`. ## ✅ Checks in this band - [ ] index.html exists - [ ] style.css exists - [ ] script.js exists - [ ] The PLAN comment is filled in with real thinking - [ ] Has an h1 naming the tool - [ ] Has two input boxes with ids - [ ] Has a #go-button - [ ] Has a #result element for the answer

# Band 2 — It computes

The spark of life: press the button, real math happens, the answer lands on the page.

## Your tool must have

1. **A working [`calculate(a, b)`](http://localhost:3031/m2-contract)** — outside the browser block, exactly where the starter put it. It takes your two input numbers and **returns** the answer. The checker calls it directly with numbers you'll never see, so honour the contract:
   - It returns a real value — not [`undefined`](http://localhost:3031/d19-zero), not [`NaN`](http://localhost:3031/d15-nan), and [`show()`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) is not [`return`](http://localhost:3031/d12-return).
   - **Different inputs give different answers.** `calculate(1, 2)` and `calculate(100, 200)` can't return the same thing. A tool with one answer for everything is a fortune cookie, not a tool.
2. **The wiring**, inside the browser block — Day 15's moves, now on your own page:
   ```js
   const a = Number(document.getElementById("pack-price").value);
   const b = Number(document.getElementById("pull-odds").value);
   document.getElementById("result").textContent = calculate(a, b);
   ```
   Read **both** boxes with [`.value`](http://localhost:3031/d15-value), convert with `Number(...)`, and put the answer in `#result` with [`textContent`](http://localhost:3031/d14-text-content).
3. A quick test in the browser: type two numbers, press the button, watch the answer change when the numbers change.

*(The raw number looks blunt sitting there alone. Band 3 teaches it to speak.)*

**Your anchors:** function `calculate(a, b)` (tested directly!), `.value` ×2, `Number(...)`, `textContent`.

## ✅ Checks in this band

- [ ] calculate(a, b) returns a real value
- [ ] Different inputs give different answers
- [ ] The click reads both inputs with .value
- [ ] Text becomes numbers with Number(...)
- [ ] The answer lands in the page (textContent)

# Band 3 — Provincial standard 🎯

This is what "a real tool" means: it makes a decision, it answers in sentences, and it looks like someone cared.

## Your tool must have

1. **A decision inside [`calculate`](http://localhost:3031/m2-contract)** — an [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if) (or `if / else if / else`) so the logic actually *thinks*. The math changes depending on the numbers: a bulk discount past a threshold, an overtime rate after 8 hours, a big-dog feed multiplier. The checker reads your function's source and looks for the `if` — it has to live **inside `calculate`**, not somewhere nearby.
2. **A working [`explain(result)`](http://localhost:3031/m2-explain)** — takes the raw answer and returns a full sentence that **contains** it:
   ```js
   explain(42)  →  "Roughly $42 to pull it. Start saving those birthday twenties."
   ```
   The checker feeds it a number and expects that number back inside a real sentence (more than just a couple of words). Then use it in the browser block: `explain(calculate(a, b))` goes into `#result`, so the page speaks human instead of blurting `42`.
3. **A styled page** — this is a Major; the CSS weeks came with you:
   - a [`body`](http://localhost:3031/d04-aiming) rule with real properties,
   - at least **two classes** you defined in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files) *and* use in the page,
   - a [`:hover`](http://localhost:3031/d07-hover) effect somewhere.

**Your anchors:** an `if` inside `calculate`, function `explain(result)` (tested directly!), a `body` rule, two classes, `:hover`.

## ✅ Checks in this band

- [ ] calculate makes a decision (has an if)
- [ ] explain(result) returns a sentence containing the result
- [ ] style.css styles the body
- [ ] Two classes defined in style.css
- [ ] Classes are used in the page
- [ ] Has a :hover effect

# Band 4 — 🚀 Bulletproof

Hand your tool to the clumsiest person you know. Empty boxes, "banana" where a number goes, button-mashing. A Level 4 tool survives all of it — *and* keeps the promise you made in your plan.

## 1. The bouncer: `validate(text)`

Finish `validate` so it answers "is this raw input text a usable number?" The checker tests it with exactly these:

| Call | Must return |
|---|---|
| `validate("")` | [`false`](http://localhost:3031/d13-else) |
| `validate("abc")` | `false` |
| `validate("7")` | `true` |

That's an empty check plus a number check — `Number("abc")` turns into [`NaN`](http://localhost:3031/d15-nan), and `Number.isNaN(...)` catches it. (Day 15's Level 4 was the dress rehearsal for this.)

## 2. Wire the bouncer in

In the browser block, run `validate` on **both** inputs' raw text **before** any math. When it says `false`, put a **friendly error in `#result`** and stop — no math, no `NaN` on the page:

```js
if (!validate(aText) || !validate(bText)) {
  // a human sentence in #result, e.g.
  // "Hold up — both boxes need real numbers before I can math."
  return;
}
```

Friendly means it sounds like you, tells the user what to fix, and never says `NaN` or [`undefined`](http://localhost:3031/d19-zero).

## 3. Ship the cool thing

Your plan's last line — "one thing that will make it cool" — comes due. Make it *move*: the checker requires a [`transition`](http://localhost:3031/d07-transition) or a [`@keyframes`](http://localhost:3031/d07-keyframes) animation in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files) (a glowing answer card, a button that springs on hover, a verdict that fades in). Your teacher also eyeballs this one: the shipped cool thing should match what your plan promised, and the error message should actually be friendly. Machines check the frame; humans check the soul.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** validation is the difference between code that works when *you* use it and code that works when *anyone* uses it. Professionals spend half their lives on this. You just started early.

**Your anchors:** function `validate(text)` (tested directly!), a `validate(...)` call in the browser block, `transition` or `@keyframes` in `style.css`.

## ✅ Checks in this band

- [ ] validate("") returns false
- [ ] validate("abc") returns false
- [ ] validate("7") returns true
- [ ] The browser block calls validate before calculating
- [ ] The cool thing moves: a transition or @keyframes in style.css

title: Day 18 — Loop-de-Loop
instructions:
Day 18 — Loop-de-Loop

The one concept: loops — repeat without repeating yourself.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — Ten lines, three lines of code
  Level 2 — The accumulator
  Level 3 — The loop that BUILDS
  Level 4 — 🧩 FizzBuzz

Files you edit: index.html, script.js, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text:
Loop-de-Loop

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — Ten lines, three lines of code

Typing `show("hi")` ten times works. It also makes you a copy-paste machine, and copy-paste machines get replaced by loops. Be the loop.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section.
2. Write a counting loop that runs **ten times** and shows something each time around:
   ```js
   for (let i = 1; i <= 10; i = i + 1) {
     show(`Rep ${i} of 10 — keep going!`);
   }
   ```
   The message is 100% yours — theme it your way. Countdown to a game release, ten laps of a track, ten levels of a dungeon, ten espresso shots. The rule is just: **a [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for) loop, with [`show(...)`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) inside it, producing ten lines.**
3. Save → refresh → ten lines appear on the page and you typed one.
4. Now the magic: change `10` to `50`. Refresh. Fifty lines. **You did not write fifty lines.** That feeling is the whole day.

> 🔍 Reading the loop head out loud helps: "start `i` at 1 — **keep going while** `i` is 10 or less — after each turn, add 1." Miss the last part and the loop never ends. If the tab freezes, that's what happened: close it, add the step, reload.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** a `for` loop with a `show(...)` call inside it.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] script.js exists and isn't empty
- [ ] A for loop has a show() call inside it
- [ ] Running your file shows at least 10 lines

# Level 2 — The accumulator

A loop that only *shows* things is half a loop. The other half **remembers** — it keeps a running total that grows every time around. Scores, streaks, totals at checkout, XP bars: all the same trick.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Make `sumTo(n)` add up every number from 1 to `n` and return the answer.
2. The pattern — **declare outside, grow inside, use after**:
   ```js
   function sumTo(n) {
     let total = 0;                          // outside: the box
     for (let i = 1; i <= n; i = i + 1) {
       total = total + i;                    // inside: it grows
     }
     return total;                           // after: hand it back
   }
   ```
   `sumTo(5)` → `1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5` → `15`.
3. Use it on something real and show the result:
   ```js
   show(`Day 1 you do 1 rep, day 2 you do 2… after 30 days that is ${sumTo(30)} reps.`);
   ```

> 😈 Yes, there is a formula that does this in one line without a loop. No, you can't use it. Gauss already had his turn in 1785 — this one's yours. The checker reads your function's source and wants to see a [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for) in there.

> 🔍 [`let total = 0;`](http://localhost:3031/d18-accumulator) **must** be outside the loop. Put it inside and you reset to zero every single turn, and `sumTo(5)` proudly returns `5`. Classic bug, worth making once.

**Your anchors:** `sumTo(n)` — already declared in the starter, your code fills it in. Must use a `for` loop.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] sumTo(5) returns 15
- [ ] sumTo(100) returns 5050
- [ ] sumTo uses a for loop (no formula shortcuts!)

# Level 3 — The loop that BUILDS

Numbers aren't the only thing that can accumulate. A **string** can grow too — and the second you can build text in a loop, you can build *page content* in a loop. This is the pattern behind every list, every leaderboard, and every star rating on every website you have ever used. It is not an exaggeration to say this is the most useful five lines on this page.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Make `buildStars(n)` return a string of `n` stars, built by a loop:
   ```js
   function buildStars(n) {
     let bar = "";                            // outside: an empty string
     for (let i = 1; i <= n; i = i + 1) {
       bar = bar + "★";                       // inside: it grows
     }
     return bar;                              // after: hand it back
   }
   ```
   Copy this exact star: **★** (that's the anchor — the checker is looking for that character).
2. `buildStars(4)` must return `★★★★`. And `buildStars(0)` must return an **empty string** — zero turns of the loop, so `bar` never grows. Check that your loop does the right thing with zero *without* adding a special case.
3. Build a rating board for **your** thing and show it:
   ```js
   show(`Snare drum   ${buildStars(5)}`);
   show(`Hi-hat       ${buildStars(3)}`);
   show(`Cowbell      ${buildStars(10)}`);
   ```
   Rate your top games, your teammates, your favourite chip flavours, every Zelda in order. Ten seconds of work, looks like a real product.

> 🔍 `return "★★★★"` passes exactly one test and fails the rest — the checker calls `buildStars` with sizes you never see, and it reads your source for a [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for). Build it, don't fake it.

**Your anchors:** `buildStars(n)` — already declared in the starter, must use a `for` loop, must use the `★` character.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] buildStars(4) returns ★★★★
- [ ] buildStars(0) returns an empty string
- [ ] buildStars uses a for loop to build the string

# Level 4 — 🧩 FizzBuzz

This is the most famous beginner programming problem in the world. Companies have used it in job interviews for twenty years, because it sorts out who can actually think in loops from who has just memorised syntax. You have had loops for about forty minutes. Let's go.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** nobody showed you the answer, and the naive first attempt is *wrong in a sneaky way* — get the order of your [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if)s backwards and 15 comes out as `"Fizz"` instead of `"FizzBuzz"`. You have to reason about which condition is the most specific and test it first. That's real algorithmic thinking, not recall.

## The problem

Make `fizzy(n)` return a **list** of results for every number from 1 to `n`:

- multiples of **3** → the word `"Fizz"`
- multiples of **5** → the word `"Buzz"`
- multiples of **both** → the word `"FizzBuzz"`
- everything else → the number itself

So `fizzy(15)` must be exactly:

```
[1, 2, "Fizz", 4, "Buzz", "Fizz", 7, 8, "Fizz", "Buzz", 11, "Fizz", 13, 14, "FizzBuzz"]
```

Those three words are **fixed anchors** — exact spelling, exact capital letters. The story you wrap around it is yours (see below).

## Do this

1. You need two tools you haven't been handed. Both are one line each on the 🔭 [reference sheet](reference/js-reference.md):
   - `%` (the **remainder**) — [`i % 3 === 0`](http://localhost:3031/d18-remainder) is how you ask "is `i` a multiple of 3?"
   - a **list** you can grow: `const results = [];` then `results.push(something)` inside the loop. Yes, that's tomorrow's lesson. Consider it a preview.
2. Loop from 1 to `n`. Each turn, decide which of the four things to push. Then return the list.
3. **The order of your `if`s is the whole puzzle.** Ask yourself which case is the pickiest, and test that one first.
4. Show it off:
   ```js
   show(fizzy(15).join("  •  "));
   ```
5. Theme the *story*, not the words: it's a drum fill counter, a combo meter, every 3rd customer gets fries and every 5th gets a drink. The returned words stay `Fizz`, `Buzz`, `FizzBuzz` — the checker compares the list character for character.

> 😈 Flex: once it works, make `fizzy(100)` and skim it. If the pattern looks right all the way down, you have out-coded a shocking number of job applicants.

**Your anchors:** `fizzy(n)` — already declared in the starter; returns an array; the strings `"Fizz"`, `"Buzz"`, `"FizzBuzz"` are exact.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] fizzy(15) returns the exact FizzBuzz list for 1–15
- [ ] fizzy(5) returns the exact list for 1–5 (n really is n)

The one concept: arrays — one variable, many values.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:

  Level 2 — Grow it, measure it
  Level 3 — The combo: loops × arrays
  Level 4 — 🧩 Find the champion

starterFiles.text:
The List Goes On

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — One box, many things

Five variables called `fav1`, `fav2`, `fav3`… is how you end up with a bad time. One **array** holds the whole line-up.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section. Fill [`favourites`](http://localhost:3031/d19-array) with **five or more** of anything you love:
   ```js
   const favourites = ["Hollow Knight", "Celeste", "Hades", "Outer Wilds", "Stardew Valley"];
   ```
   Songs, sneakers, wrestlers, bosses you've beaten, hockey teams, chip flavours. Totally yours — the checker only counts them.
2. Now read **one** of them out of the box. Slots are numbered from **zero**, so the first thing lives at [`[0]`](http://localhost:3031/d19-zero):
   ```js
   show(`Number one with a bullet: ${favourites[0]}`);
   ```
3. Save → refresh → your number one appears.
4. Poke it: try `favourites[1]`, then `favourites[2]`, then `favourites[99]`. That last one says `undefined` — the machine's way of saying "there's nothing in that slot."

> 🔍 The name `favourites` is the anchor — keep the spelling exactly (yes, with the Canadian `u`). The five things inside are yours forever.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** `favourites` — already declared in the starter, you fill it; the first item read as `favourites[0]`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] favourites is a list holding 5 or more things
- [ ] The first item is read with favourites[0]

# Level 2 — Grow it, measure it

Lists aren't statues. Every "add to cart", every "follow", every new message in a group chat is a [`.push()`](http://localhost:3031/d19-push) onto an array somewhere. And every "3 items · $42" is a [`.length`](http://localhost:3031/d19-length).

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Add at least **two** more things to [`favourites`](http://localhost:3031/d19-array) — *without* retyping the original list:
   ```js
   favourites.push("Tunic");
   favourites.push("Animal Well");
   ```
   `.push()` always adds to the **end**. The list is now longer than you wrote it.
2. Ask the list how big it is, and show that:
   ```js
   show(`The shelf now holds ${favourites.length} games.`);
   ```
3. Refresh. The number should match what you'd count by hand.

> 🤯 `const favourites` and you can still `.push()` to it? Yes. [`const`](http://localhost:3031/d10-const-let) locks the **box**, not what's inside it. You can't hand it a whole new list, but you can absolutely add things to the one it's holding. Weird, true, and worth remembering.

> 🔍 It's `.length` with no brackets — it's a fact about the list, not something you call. `favourites.length()` is an error.

**Your anchors:** `favourites.push(…)` and `favourites.length`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The list grows with favourites.push(…)
- [ ] The list is measured with favourites.length

# Level 3 — The combo: loops × arrays

Yesterday's loop. Today's array. Together they are the four most useful lines in web development — this is literally how every feed, playlist, and leaderboard on the internet gets onto your screen.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Loop the whole list and show every item:
   ```js
   for (let i = 0; i < favourites.length; i = i + 1) {
     show(`${i + 1}. ${favourites[i]}`);
   }
   ```
   Read the loop head out loud: **start at 0**, keep going **while `i` is less than length**, step by 1. That's the standard array loop — you will write it a thousand times.
   *(The `i + 1` in the message is just so humans see "1." instead of "0." — humans are weird about counting.)*
2. Now `firstAndLast(arr)`. It gets handed **any** array and returns a two-item array: the first thing and the last thing.
   ```js
   function firstAndLast(arr) {
     return [arr[0], arr[???]];
   }
   ```
   The first slot is easy. The last one is the thinking bit: **the last slot is always [`length - 1`](http://localhost:3031/d19-length).** Ten items → slots 0 to 9. Work out what goes in the `???`.
3. Use it on your own list and show the result:
   ```js
   const ends = firstAndLast(favourites);
   show(`Oldest love: ${ends[0]} — newest obsession: ${ends[1]}`);
   ```

> 🔍 The checker hands `firstAndLast` arrays it never showed you, including a **2-item** one where the first and last are the only two things there. If you hardcoded `arr[6]`, you're toast. Use `length - 1`.

**Your anchors:** a [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for) loop with [`show(...)`](http://localhost:3031/d10-show) inside it; `firstAndLast(arr)` — already declared in the starter, returns `[first, last]`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] A for loop has a show() call inside it
- [ ] firstAndLast(["a","b","c"]) returns ["a","c"]
- [ ] firstAndLast([1,2]) returns [1,2] (a 2-item list still has ends)

# Level 4 — 🧩 Find the champion

There is a built-in helper called [`Math.max`](http://localhost:3031/d19-biggest) that finds the biggest number for you. **It is banned today.** You're going to build it yourself, because the pattern hiding inside it — "remember the best one so far" — is one you'll reuse for the rest of your life: high scores, closest enemy, cheapest option, longest streak.

> 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** the computer cannot glance at a list and spot the biggest. It can only look at **one number at a time**, in order, with a terrible memory. So how do you end up knowing the answer? That question is the whole puzzle, and nobody has shown you the answer. Also, the obvious first attempt has a trap in it (see the negatives test below).

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 4** section. Make `biggest(arr)` return the largest number in `arr`.
2. Think about how **you** would do it if someone read the numbers out to you one at a time and you weren't allowed to write anything down except **one** number:
   - you'd remember a "best so far"
   - each new number: is this better than my best so far? If yes, that's my new best.
   - when the numbers run out, your "best so far" *is* the answer.

   That's a variable outside the loop and an [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if) inside it. You already own both tools.
3. **The trap.** Starting with `let best = 0;` feels natural and it is wrong. Run `biggest([-5, -2, -9])` — every number in there is *worse* than 0, so nothing ever beats your starting value and you confidently return `0`, which isn't even in the list. Fix: start `best` at something that's guaranteed to be in the list. (Hint: how about the first thing in it?)
4. Use it on something real from your world and show it:
   ```js
   const hoursPlayed = [42, 17, 96, 61, 8];
   show(`Longest playthrough: ${biggest(hoursPlayed)} hours. Worth it.`);
   ```

> 😈 Flex (not checked): now write `smallest(arr)`. It's the same function with one character changed, and realising that is the point of the whole exercise.

**Your anchors:** `biggest(arr)` — already declared in the starter, tested with arrays you've never seen, and **`Math.max` is not allowed anywhere inside it** (the checker reads your function's source).

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] biggest([3,9,2]) returns 9
- [ ] biggest([-5,-2,-9]) returns -2 (all negatives!)
- [ ] biggest does it without Math.max

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:

starterFiles.text:
The Collection

Rename this whole page after whatever you collect.

# Level 1 — The array draws itself Today's page is a **collection tracker**. Cards, sneakers, books, PRs at the gym, bands you've seen live, Pokémon you've caught — pick one thing you collect and make this page about it. Rename the [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1), rewrite the tagline, own it. The rule that makes it all work: **the array is the truth, the page is just a picture of it.** ## Do this 1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), replace the Level 1 comment with an **empty** bullet list: ```html
    ``` Empty. On purpose. You will never type a [`
  • `](http://localhost:3031/d02-li) in this file — your code makes them. 2. In [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js), put three real things in the array up top: ```js let items = ["Foil Charizard", "Starter Pack", "That One Bent Pikachu"]; ``` 3. Inside the **browser block**, write [`render()`](http://localhost:3031/d20-render) — the five-move pattern: ```js const listEl = document.getElementById("item-list"); function render() { listEl.innerHTML = ""; // wipe the old picture for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i = i + 1) { // loop the truth const row = document.createElement("li"); // make an element row.textContent = items[i]; // fill it listEl.appendChild(row); // stick it on the page } } ``` 4. **Call it.** At the bottom of the browser block, write `render();` — a function that never gets called cooks zero food. 5. Save → refresh → your three things are on the page. Now add a fourth to the array and refresh. The page updated and you never touched the HTML. That's the whole day. > 🔍 [`createElement`](http://localhost:3031/d20-create-append) makes a brick; `appendChild` clicks it onto the build. Forget the second line and your items exist in memory, invisible, forever. > ⚠️ Everything that touches `document` goes **inside** the browser block. The ⚙️ lines top and bottom belong to the checker — leave them alone. **Your anchors:** [`
      `](http://localhost:3031/d20-item-list) (empty in the HTML), the [`items`](http://localhost:3031/d20-truth) array, and a [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for) loop running to [`items.length`](http://localhost:3031/d20-count-clear). ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] The page has an empty `
        ` - [ ] Your code builds page content (createElement or innerHTML) - [ ] A for loop runs across items.length

# Level 2 — Type it, click it, it's there

Right now only *you* can add things, by editing code. Time to let a human do it. This is the moment your page stops being a page and starts being an **app**.

## Do this

1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), inside `#controls`, add the input box and the button:
   ```html
   <input id="item-input" type="text" placeholder="Card name…">
   <button id="add-button">Add to binder</button>
   ```
   Label the button however you like — "Add to binder", "Cop it", "+ LOG IT". The **ids** are the anchors; the words are yours.
2. In [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js), **outside** the browser block, finish [`addItem`](http://localhost:3031/d20-add). It gets a list and a thing, puts the thing on the end, and hands the list back:
   ```js
   function addItem(list, item) {
     list.push(item);
     return list;
   }
   ```
   Yesterday's [`.push()`](http://localhost:3031/d19-push), wearing a function. The checker calls this directly with lists you've never seen.
3. Inside the browser block, wire the button up. Read → add → redraw:
   ```js
   const inputEl = document.getElementById("item-input");

   document.getElementById("add-button").addEventListener("click", function () {
     const typed = inputEl.value;      // .value = whatever the human typed
     items = addItem(items, typed);
     inputEl.value = "";               // empty the box, ready for the next one
     render();
   });
   ```
4. Save → refresh → type something → click. **It appears.** Do it five times. You built that.

> 🔍 Notice you never touched the page in that click handler — you changed the array and called [`render()`](http://localhost:3031/d20-render). One drawing function, called from everywhere. That discipline is why real apps don't fall apart.

> 😈 Flex (not checked): if the box is empty, don't add anything. `if (typed !== "") { … }` — one line, and your app stops collecting ghosts.

**Your anchors:** `<input id="item-input">`, `<button id="add-button">`, `addItem(list, item)` — already declared in the starter.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] The page has an `<input id="item-input">`
- [ ] The page has an #add-button
- [ ] addItem(["a"], "b") returns ["a","b"]
- [ ] Your code reads what the user typed with .value
- [ ] The new item lands on the page (appendChild or innerHTML)

# Level 3 — The actual tracker An app that only adds is a toy. A **tracker** tells you how many you've got and lets you start over. Two small features, and suddenly the thing looks finished. ## Do this 1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), under the list, add somewhere for the number to live: ```html

0 cards in the binder

``` And put a clear button in `#controls`: ```html ``` 2. **The count.** Don't add a whole new function for it — put it at the **bottom of [`render()`](http://localhost:3031/d20-render)**, so it can never fall out of sync with the list: ```js document.getElementById("count").textContent = items.length + " cards in the binder"; ``` Word it your way ("3 pairs · 1 grail", "12 books read"), but the number must come from [`items.length`](http://localhost:3031/d20-count-clear), not from a variable you increment yourself. 3. **The clear.** Change the truth, then redraw — same as always: ```js document.getElementById("clear-button").addEventListener("click", function () { items = []; render(); }); ``` 4. Test it like a user: add four things (count goes 1, 2, 3, 4), clear (count goes 0, list empties), add two more. Everything should stay in step, because everything is drawn from one array by one function. > 🔍 Why put the count inside `render()`? Because then there is exactly **one** place in your whole program where the page gets updated. Track the count separately and one day you'll clear the list and the counter will still smugly say 7. Real bug, real apps, all the time. **Your anchors:** `id="count"` updated from `items.length`, `

starterFiles.text:
Everything's an Object

Everything your script.js shows appears below:

# Level 1 — One thing, many named parts

Pick the single most important thing in your world — a character, a player, a song, a car, a dog — and give the machine a proper file on it.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section and fill the curly braces with **at least three named parts**. One of them must be called `name`:
   ```js
   const myThing = {
     name: "Sir Reginald Toast",
     role: "Breakfast Knight",
     power: 9000,
     catchphrase: "Butter side up. Always."
   };
   ```
   The parts are yours to invent: `hp`, `price`, `position`, `album`, `emoji`, `topSpeed`. Numbers go without quotes, text goes with.
2. Reach inside with a **dot** and show what you find:
   ```js
   show(myThing.name);
   show(`${myThing.name} fights as a ${myThing.role}.`);
   ```
3. Save → refresh. The machine now knows your thing part by part.

> 🔍 [`myThing.name`](http://localhost:3031/d21-my-thing) gives you what's *inside* the box called `name`. `"myThing.name"` in quotes just prints that text. Dots reach in; quotes do not.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** `myThing` — already declared in the starter, your parts fill it in. Needs 3+ parts and one called `name`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] myThing has at least 3 named parts
- [ ] One of those parts is called name, with text in it
- [ ] You read a part back out with a dot, like myThing.name

# Level 2 — A recipe that reads inside

Day 12 gave you functions. Day 11 gave you template literals. Today they team up: one recipe that takes **any** object and writes a sentence about it.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Make [`describe`](http://localhost:3031/d21-describe) build one sentence using **at least two** of the object's parts:
   ```js
   function describe(thing) {
     return `${thing.name} rolls in with a power level of ${thing.power}. Do not test them.`;
   }
   ```
   The wording is 100% yours. The only rule: two different parts must show up in the sentence.
2. Try it on the object you built at Level 1:
   ```js
   show(describe(myThing));
   ```
3. Now the flex: hand it an object you invented on the spot.
   ```js
   show(describe({ name: "Rex", power: 9000 }));
   ```
   Same recipe, brand new subject. That's the whole point of parameters.

> 🔍 The checker hands `describe` objects you've never seen — ones with `name` and `power` on them. If you typed your own thing's details *inside* the function, every call gives the same sentence and the test catches it instantly. Use `thing.` to reach in.

**Your anchors:** `describe(thing)` — already declared in the starter. Must use `thing.name` and one more part.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] describe() puts the thing's name in the sentence
- [ ] describe() uses a SECOND part too (the 9000 shows up)
- [ ] describe() gives back a real sentence, not a scrap

# Level 3 — The whole roster

One object is a character. An **array of objects** is a roster, a leaderboard, a playlist, an inventory, a starting eleven. This is the shape real apps are made of — and today you build one and put it on the page.

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 3** section. Fill [`roster`](http://localhost:3031/d21-roster) with **at least three objects**, each with a `name` and the same kinds of parts you used in [`describe`](http://localhost:3031/d21-describe):
   ```js
   const roster = [
     { name: "Sir Reginald Toast", role: "Breakfast Knight", power: 9000 },
     { name: "Captain Cereal", role: "Milk Wizard", power: 7200 },
     { name: "The Waffle", role: "Absolute Unit", power: 8100 }
   ];
   ```
   Your cast, your world: five players, four songs, three horses, eight Pokémon.
2. Loop over it with a Day 18 [`for`](http://localhost:3031/d18-for) loop and describe **every** member. Use `roster[i]` so the loop hands each object to your Level 2 recipe:
   ```js
   for (let i = 0; i < roster.length; i = i + 1) {
     show(describe(roster[i]));
   }
   ```
3. Save → refresh. Three lines of code just wrote a whole roster page. Add a fourth character to the array — no new code — and refresh again. It just appears.

> 🔍 `roster[1]` is an object. `roster[1].name` is that object's name. Number first (which one), then dot (which part).

**Your anchors:** `roster` — already declared in the starter; 3+ objects, each with a `name`. Your loop must call `describe(roster[i])`.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] roster holds 3+ objects and every one of them has a name
- [ ] A loop walks the roster
- [ ] describe() is called on roster members, like describe(roster[i])

Roll the Dice

# Level 1 — The die

Board games, battle sims, loot drops, damage rolls. It all starts with one honest six-sided die.

## Do this

1. Open [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). Find the **Level 1** section and build the die, one layer at a time:
   ```js
   function roll() {
     return Math.floor(Math.random() * 6) + 1;
   }
   ```
   - [`Math.random() * 6`](http://localhost:3031/d22-die) → a decimal from 0 up to just under 6
   - `Math.floor(...)` → chops the decimal off: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 or 5
   - `+ 1` → 1 to 6. A die.
2. Roll it a couple of times and show the results:
   ```js
   show(`You rolled a ${roll()}.`);
   show(`The dragon rolled a ${roll()}.`);
   ```
3. Refresh. Refresh again. Different numbers, same code. Best of three?

> 🔍 Forget the `+ 1` and you'll roll a 0 sooner or later. Use `Math.round` instead of `Math.floor` and your 1s and 6s show up half as often as everything else — a loaded die. Details matter here.

> ⚠️ The two ⚙️ lines at the top and the ⚙️ line at the bottom of `script.js` belong to the checker. Leave them alone.

**Your anchors:** `roll()` — already declared in the starter. Whole numbers, 1 to 6, and it must actually vary.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] roll() always gives a whole number from 1 to 6
- [ ] roll() actually varies (30 rolls are not all the same)
- [ ] You show a roll on the page

# Level 2 — The hype-line generator

Dice are numbers. The real magic is picking a random **thing** — a random song, a random opponent, a random insult. Same trick, one upgrade: instead of `* 6`, use [`* list.length`](http://localhost:3031/d22-pick).

## Do this

1. Find the **Level 2** section. Make `pickRandom` hand back one random member of whatever list it's given:
   ```js
   function pickRandom(list) {
     return list[Math.floor(Math.random() * list.length)];
   }
   ```
   `list.length` (not the number 4!) means it works on a list of 3 or 300.
2. Build a small array of hype lines about **your** thing, then show a random one:
   ```js
   const hypeLines = [
     "certified breakfast royalty",
     "unbeatable before 9am",
     "banned in three cafeterias",
     "smells like victory and maple syrup"
   ];

   show(`Today's hype: your thing is ${pickRandom(hypeLines)}.`);
   ```
   Four or more lines, as unhinged as you like. Roasts, compliments, fortunes, band names, excuses — your call.
3. Refresh five times. Five different hype lines from one line of code.

> 🔍 Why `Math.floor(Math.random() * list.length)` never breaks: [`Math.random()`](http://localhost:3031/d22-random) never quite reaches 1, so the biggest position it can produce is [`length - 1`](http://localhost:3031/d19-length) — exactly the last slot. Positions start at 0, remember.

**Your anchors:** `pickRandom(list)` — already declared in the starter, and it must actually get *called* somewhere with your own list.

## ✅ You pass when

- [ ] pickRandom() always gives back a member of the list
- [ ] pickRandom() actually varies (20 picks are not all the same)
- [ ] pickRandom is actually called, not just written

title: Day 23 — Game On
instructions:
Day 23 — Game On

The one concept: game state — variables that remember.

Today's four activities — your level is the highest one where everything below it is green too:
  Level 1 — The loop of joy
  Level 2 — The win condition
  Level 3 — A full game
  Level 4 — ⏱️ Beat the clock

Files you edit: index.html, script.js, style.css
Full instructions are in level1.md–level4.md, right here in this folder.
Concept names in them are clickable — they open the slide that teaches it.

starterFiles.text: Game On

# Level 3 — A full game Win once and your page is a museum piece — frozen on the victory screen forever. Real games let you go again. Add a message box, a reset button, and you have a genuinely finished, genuinely playable game. ## Do this 1. In [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html), add the two remaining pieces: ```html

Toast some bread. Save the world.

``` 2. In [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js), finish [`resetGame`](http://localhost:3031/d23-reset) — outside the browser block, with the rest of the brain: ```js function resetGame() { score = 0; } ``` `score = 0;` — no [`let`](http://localhost:3031/d10-const-let). You're refilling the existing box, not building a new one. 3. Wire the reset button inside the browser block. Reset means *everything* goes back, not just the number: ```js document.getElementById("reset-button").addEventListener("click", function () { resetGame(); document.getElementById("score-display").textContent = getScore(); document.getElementById("game-message").textContent = "Fresh loaf. Go again."; }); ``` 4. Polish move: once [`hasWon`](http://localhost:3031/d23-win) is true, stop the click button from adding more points — wrap the adding part in `if (hasWon(getScore()) === false) { ... }`. Winning should actually *end* something. 5. Playtest it: click to the goal, read your win message, hit reset, do it again. Hand your laptop to the person next to you. If they play it twice, you built a game. > 🔍 Repeating `document.getElementById("score-display").textContent = getScore();` in two places? Bottle it into a little `paintScore()` function inside the browser block and call it from both. Day 12 skills never stop paying. **Your anchors:** `#reset-button` and `#game-message` in the HTML; `resetGame()` in the script; `hasWon` actually used in your wiring. ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] resetGame() puts the score back to 0 - [ ] The page has a #reset-button - [ ] The page has a #game-message - [ ] hasWon is actually used in the browser block, not just written

# Level 4 — ⏱️ Beat the clock Your game now has a clock. Beat the GOAL before it hits zero. Nothing raises the stakes like a timer. Until now, *every* line of your code has run because a human did something — the page loaded, a button got clicked. A timer is the first code you write that runs **on its own**. > 🤔 **Why this is Level 4:** this is a reference dive — [`setInterval`](http://localhost:3031/d23-timer) was never taught in class. You have to find it in the 🔭 [reference sheet](reference/js-reference.md), work out what its two arguments mean (a function, and a number of milliseconds), and split the job in two: `countdown` is a pure tick function the checker can test on its own, while `setInterval` is the browser-only engine that calls it once a second. Recognising which half goes inside the browser block and which half stays outside is the real challenge here. ## Do this 1. **First the tick.** Outside the browser block, finish `countdown` — it takes the seconds left and hands back one second less, but **never goes below zero**: - `countdown(5)` → `4` - `countdown(0)` → `0` That's an [`if`](http://localhost:3031/d13-if) and two returns. Test it in your head before you build the clock; a broken tick makes a very confusing timer. 2. Add a place to show the time in [`index.html`](http://localhost:3031/d01-index-html): ```html

Time left: 15

``` 3. **Then the engine.** Inside the browser block, keep a `timeLeft` variable and hand `setInterval` a function to run every 1000 milliseconds: ```js let timeLeft = 15; setInterval(function () { timeLeft = countdown(timeLeft); document.getElementById("timer-display").textContent = `Time left: ${timeLeft}`; if (timeLeft === 0) { document.getElementById("game-message").textContent = `⏰ Time! You got ${getScore()} of ${GOAL}.`; } }, 1000); ``` 4. Now make the rules honest — inside your click handler, only add a point when `timeLeft > 0`. Clicking after time is up should do exactly nothing. 5. Don't forget the reset button: it should put `timeLeft` back to the start too, or your rematch lasts zero seconds. 6. Playtest. Is 15 seconds for 12 points too easy? Too brutal? Tune the two numbers until it's *just* winnable. That tuning — right there — is game design. > 😈 Bonus flex (not checked): flash the timer red under 5 seconds with `.style.color`, or make the goal harder each time someone wins. **Your anchors:** `countdown(n)` — already declared in the starter (tested directly!); `setInterval` used in [`script.js`](http://localhost:3031/d10-script-js). ## ✅ You pass when - [ ] script.js uses setInterval( ) for the clock - [ ] countdown(5) returns 4 - [ ] countdown(0) returns 0 (the clock never goes negative)

         one an id you choose, kebab-case, named for what it does. -->

         player learns — hits, misses, wins, losses — lands in here. -->

# Band 1 — The cabinet Before a game can be fun it has to *exist*. Band 1 builds the cabinet: a plan, a page with buttons on it, a place for the game to speak, and the one function that starts a run. ## Your game must have 1. **A real plan** in the [``](http://localhost:3031/m1-plan) comment — all four lines, actual thoughts, not "idk". Especially **How you lose** — that's the line that makes it a game instead of a toy. 2. An [`

`](http://localhost:3031/d01-h1) with your game's name (put it in the [``](http://localhost:3031/d01-title) too). 3. **At least two play buttons** — the verbs of your game. Attack and Flee. Guess High and Guess Low. Dodge and Catch. Ids are yours to name, kebab-case: ```html <button id="attack-button">⚔️ Attack</button> <button id="flee-button">🏃 Flee</button> ``` 4. **The element your game talks through**, with this exact id: ```html <p id="game-message">Press Attack to start the run.</p> ``` Every hit, miss, win and loss gets announced in here. It's your game's mouth. 5. **The restart button**, with this exact id: ```html <button id="restart-button">🔄 Restart</button> ``` It doesn't have to work yet. Band 3 makes it work. 6. **[`newGame()`](http://localhost:3031/m3-contract) finished** — the first piece of the contract. It hands back a *brand new* state object: ```js function newGame() { return { score: 0, lives: 3 }; } ``` The checker only looks at `score === 0` in this band. But put the lives in now anyway — Band 4 comes for them. Nothing plays yet. That's fine. Band 1 is the cabinet, Band 2 is the power cord. **Your anchors:** `<!-- PLAN: ... -->`, `<h1>`, two play buttons (your ids), `id="game-message"`, [`id="restart-button"`](http://localhost:3031/m3-band3), function `newGame()` (tested directly!). ## ✅ Checks in this band - [ ] index.html exists - [ ] style.css exists - [ ] script.js exists - [ ] The PLAN comment is filled in with real thinking - [ ] Has an h1 naming the game - [ ] Has at least two buttons to play with - [ ] Has a #game-message element the game talks through - [ ] Has a #restart-button - [ ] newGame() returns a fresh state object with score 0

# Band 3 — Provincial standard 🎯 You can win

A game you can't win is a fidget toy. This band adds the finish line, the button that lets someone play again, and the paint job that makes people *want* to.

## Your game must have

1. **[`WINSCORE`](http://localhost:3031/m3-contract) — a number bigger than 0.** Your finish line. Pick something reachable in 30–60 seconds of clicking; you can tune it after you playtest.
   ```js
   const WINSCORE = 20;
   ```
2. **A working `hasWon(state)`.** One line, no drama:
   ```js
   function hasWon(state) {
     return state.score >= WINSCORE;
   }
   ```
   The checker tests it twice: [`true`](http://localhost:3031/d13-else) when the score has reached `WINSCORE`, and `false` for a brand new game. ([`>=`](http://localhost:3031/d13-compare) not `===` — overshooting the target still wins.)
3. **The win actually announced.** After every click, ask the question and say something worth reading:
   ```js
   if (hasWon(state)) {
     document.getElementById("game-message").textContent = "🏆 ARENA CLEARED! You are the champion.";
   }
   ```
4. **The restart button wired up.** Throw the old state away and start clean — score back to 0, message reset, everything on screen redrawn:
   ```js
   restartButton.addEventListener("click", function () {
     state = newGame();
     // …then redraw the score and reset the message
   });
   ```
   Test it properly: win the game, hit Restart, and check that the score is *actually* 0 again and not stuck on 20.
5. **A styled page** — this is a Major, and the CSS weeks came with you:
   - a [`body`](http://localhost:3031/d04-aiming) rule with real properties,
   - at least **two classes** you defined in [`style.css`](http://localhost:3031/d04-two-files) *and* use in the page,
   - a [`:hover`](http://localhost:3031/d07-hover) effect (buttons that don't react to your mouse feel dead).

**Your anchors:** `WINSCORE` (tested directly!), function `hasWon(state)` (tested directly!), a `restart-button` listener, a `body` rule, two classes, `:hover`.

## ✅ Checks in this band

- [ ] WINSCORE is a number bigger than 0
- [ ] hasWon is true once the score reaches WINSCORE
- [ ] hasWon is false for a brand new game
- [ ] The restart button is wired up in script.js
- [ ] style.css styles the body
- [ ] Has a :hover effect
- [ ] Two classes defined in style.css
- [ ] Classes are used in the page
